responseSet.authorMast: Renate M. Kohlheimer and  Michael Wininger
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter From Vienna

Renate M. Kohlheimer and Michael Wininger

Michael Wininger

renate.kohlheimer@wpv.at
1010 Vienna, Spiegelgasse 21, Vienna, Austria

Michael Wininger  

Vienna Psychoanalytical Society, Shadows of Heritage, development of new psychoanalytic currents alongside with Vienna's own tradition

The EPF 35th Annual Conference will be held in Vienna in July 2022 after it was postponed in 2020 due to the pandemic. All the greater is the pleasure to welcome you to the EPF Congress with the theme of Ideals this summer. The Vienna Psychoanalytical Society (WPV, Wiener Psychoanalytische Vereinigung) and the Vienna Psychoanalytical Association (WAP, Wiener Arbeitskreis für Psychoanalyse) would like to extend a warm invitation and welcome to Vienna, the birthplace of Psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: Avatar of Desire?: Virtual Space of Possibility in Video and Telephone Analysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Avatar of Desire?: Virtual Space of Possibility in Video and Telephone Analysis**

Stefanie Sedlacek

Stefanie Sedlacek

dr.stefanie.sedlacek@gmx.de
Germany

Stefanie Sedlacek  

Remote analysis, setting, physical pain, embodied memory, physical Countertransference

In this paper the relationship of the analytic pair in treatments with the aid of digital media is investigated as to how it is experienced in a double virtual relationship in which the other is not physically present. The author argues that ‘remote analysis’ promotes a regression to idealised object imagoes because it is located in a double virtual space—that of the medium and that of psychoanalysis. Both spaces involve an attack on or at least a destabilisation of the defence and so target the domain of the virtually immortal wishful impulses (Freud, 1933, p. 73). With reference to a case example, the author discusses how previously suppressed biographical memories that had only been perceived as physical experience and pain could be lived through in double virtuality and experienced so that they became undeniable for the patient and accessible to working through. As a result emotional insight and deeper understanding were achieved. The psychic reality of the unfulfilled longing became palpable and could be overcome in the virtual space of remote analysis and analysis in person.

responseSet.title: The ‘Community Turn’: Relational citizenship in the Psychoanalytic Community Collaboratory®
responseSet.authorMast: Jane A. Hassinger and  Billie A. Pivnick
responseSet.authorCitation: Hassinger, J. A. & Pivnick, B. A.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The ‘Community Turn’: Relational citizenship in the Psychoanalytic Community Collaboratory®

Jane Hassinger and Billie Pivnick

Billie Pivnick

drbilliepivnick@gmail.com
San Francisco

New York

Billie Pivnick  

Drawn from five years of experience in the web-based Psychoanalytic Community Collaboratory, this paper explores implications of the 'community turn' in psychoanalysis for roles, methods, clinical theory, and training. With participants from many parts of the world, the Collaboratory has become a creative generator of projects including documentary films, community memorial initiatives, and mental health interventions in highly stressed communities. The Collaboratory's unique pedagogy offers valuable experiential learning about the complex intersubjective dynamics common to group and community life. Through reflection on the interpersonal dynamics of three critical incidents, we illustrate the interplay of intra-psychic and political aspects of identity--what we have termed 'relational citizenship', an intersubjective self-state in which the individual and the sociopolitical are psychically linked and where the challenges of identifying with and belonging to one or more collectivities are recognized and negotiated.

responseSet.title: Letter from the Ukrainian Psychoanalytic Society
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter from the Ukrainian Psychoanalytic Society

Oleksandra Mirza and Igor Romanov

Igor Romanov

UPS President

Head of UPS Training Committee

Igor Romanov  

Dear Colleagues,

We are grateful to the IJP for the opportunity to address the psychoanalytic community and to speak about the situation in the Ukrainian Psychoanalytic Society (UPS) and in Ukraine in general.

The UPS, an IPA study group, was established in its current format in 2016; however, the prior history of the psychoanalytic movement in Ukraine is much longer (Pushrakeva and Romanov 2000). Currently, there are 16 direct IPA members in our society, of which four are training analysts and two more are also authorized to conduct training analysis; 28 candidates are being trained at the UPS. At the IPA, our group is overseen by the Sponsorship Committee, which comprises Ingo Focke, Solvi Kristiansen and Alexander Janssen.

Until very recently, we could assume that the development of the UPS, and the development of psychoanalysis in Ukraine in compliance with IPA standards, was progressing quite successfully. The Society had developed training regulations; the third group of the theoretical programme for candidates was already underway; the UPS held annual conferences, which were attended by both analysts from Western countries and colleagues from neighbouring ones - Russia, Belarus, Moldova, Armenia, Georgia, Lithuania, etc. The plan was to set up a child psychoanalysis section. In Ukraine, there is already one child psychoanalyst, while two more direct IPA members and one candidate are completing their studies in the framework of the IPA project on child and adolescent psychoanalysis under the auspices of the European Psychoanalytic Institute. Members of the society and regional groups conducted multi-year programmes with international colleagues: the Kleinian Seminar with the support of Melanie Klein Trust (see Teaching in Ukraine); the Collaboration Programme with German Psychoanalytic Institutes (including the Karl Abraham Berlin Psychoanalytic Institute, Mainz Psychoanalytic Institute and Tübingen Institute); an Infant Observation Programme supported by trainers from the Tavistock Clinic; a Parent Infant Psychotherapy training programme in collaboration with the Anna Freud Centre, the Psycho-Oncology training course (Christa Hack); and the Training for Trainers programme (Thijs de Wolf).

responseSet.title: The Institution, Mental Disability and the Psychoanalyst: Prospects Presented by a Revival of Intersubjective Receptivity in Groups
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Institution, Mental Disability and the Psychoanalyst: Prospects Presented by a Revival of Intersubjective Receptivity in Groups

Armelle Hours

Armelle Hours

dr.armellehours@orange.fr
Foyer de l’Arc, Mornant
3 place des cordeliers, 69002 Lyon, France

Armelle Hours  

Group; intersubjectivity; disability; mental disability; institution; creativity

This presentation aims at giving an account of a novel group project, conducted with adults with mental and/or psychic disabilities. The evolution of post-war psychiatry in France is marked by several important changes. The development of psychotherapy in institutions asserts the role of psychoanalysis in the institutional sphere. We then will describe the environmental contexts for the reception and support of the mentally disabled subject as it currently exists in France, highlighting their effects on psychic disability. This novel project takes place in a medico-social institution, a residential home in which live eight adults with disabilities, women and men, aged 19 to 55, and at a particular moment in institutional history. Sustained by several mediations, it is first through the support of a jigsaw puzzle project that the experience is initiated. Then a writing workshop will be organised, whereby residues of proceedings will find a form in which to be recorded. The objective will be the implementation of intersubjective space, despite the impediment identified at the level of individual subjective psyches. The effectiveness of the methods used can be detected in the intersubjective connections, allowing the renewal of symbolization and creativity.

A Beholder’s Share: Essays on Winnicott and the Psychoanalytic Imagination. By Dodi Goldman. London and New York, Routledge, 2017, pp.205, £ 36.99. ISBN: 9781138289369

Pina Antinucci

Pina Antinucci

pina.antinucci@gmail.com
112A Shirland Road, London W92BT, UK

Pina Antinucci  

How can we think the movement of life within any culturally specific symbolic framework? First, thought emerges from and follows experience, in the après-coup instituted by potential temporality; moreover, any representational reflection is conditional on the categorical givens proffered by language. These prerequisites of necessity apply to psychoanalytic conceptualizations if, when all is said and done, the psychoanalyst is a sentient and speaking being, and hence subjected to the symbolic order.

The French philosopher and sinologist Francois Jullien interrogates the assumption of naturalness of the Western categorical binaries - Being and non-Being, life and death, good and evil, subject and object, and similar chains of opposites - from a position outside the philosophical Western tradition. From his perspective of scholar of the Chinese language, classical and modern poetry, philosophy and military strategy, he observes that, unlike the Chinese language, whose structure accommodates transformations and renders them thinkable, since Plato Western thought has not developed the conceptualizations apt to capture 231

responseSet.title: The Sources of the Concept of Extension in the Work of W. R. Bion
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Sources of the Concept of Extension in the Work of W. R. Bion

Didier Houzel

Didier Houzel

houzeld4@gmail.com
Normandy, France
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-9348-3846

France

Didier Houzel  

Abstraction; α-function; autism; Bion’s grid; countertransference; symbolization

Bion frequently resorts to mathematical thought in his use of abstractions that account for “elements of psychoanalysis”; among these, the concept of extension plays a central role. The author's hypothesis is that Bion borrowed the concept of extension from Alfred North Whitehead, whose book An Introduction to Mathematics, published in 1911, Bion read. The examples that both Bion and Whitehead use to illustrate the process of extension are almost the same. It is on the basis of this concept of extension that Bion accounts for the psychic growth of each individual, and for the analytic process in terms of transference interpretations in three registers - the senses, the personal myth of the patient, and passion. Bion's description of extension in the psychoanalytical field helps to make explicit some aspects of the psychoanalytic process, with an emphasis on the intuition of the analyst. The author's argument is illustrated with clinical material from the analysis of an autistic child.

responseSet.title: Umbilical Phantoms
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Umbilical Phantoms

Siri Hustvedt

Siri Hustvedt

jen.dougherty07@gmail.com
544 2nd Street, Brooklyn, NY, 11215

Siri Hustvedt  

The detachment of the subject from the object that establishes the room for abstract thought originates in the cutting of the umbilical cord.

Aby Warburg (Gombrich 1986, 246)

I have long been fascinated by between states and places and the role they play in making art. I am convinced the psychoanalytic healing and art making are born in the between, a mix of here and there, me and you, dreams and waking reality, inside and outside, feeling and symbol, and the roots of this between reality pre-date the thinking subject but live on in the rhythms of our bodies in relation to other bodies and the world. By mix I mean that the borders we have erected between these binary concepts inevitably buckle, especially when they are applied to infantile experience, a time before such conceptual divisions are symbolized.

Transference and countertransference, which together constitute the electric between zone of analysis, have been theorized in various ways over time, but from the beginning Freud made it clear that transference is not confined to the analytic room, but is part of everyday life. Unrecognized patterns repeat themselves in relation to other people and may turn one person into another or create a mingled being, a fictional or dream character, who plays a role in the story being written and then vanishes or reappears in another guise.

D.W. Winnicott reconfigured Freud’s “intermediate area between illness and real life” (Freud 1955d, 154) into potential space, the rambunctious arena of human play, creativity and culture (Winnicott 2005). In a lecture I gave in Vienna in 2011 for the Freud Foundation (Hustvedt 2012, 196-222), I noted that James Strachey’s translation of Freud’s Tummelplatz as playground may have sparked Winnicott’s metamorphosis of this zone. Winnicott (1971, 100) defines the potential space of cultural experience as “between the individual and the environment (originally the object)”, a space that is not inside the person but not entirely outside them either. This area opens up in babyhood and is part of healthy development, an illusion that is not necessarily pathological. Winnicott refers to this space “as that which initially joins and separates the baby and the mother” (103, emphasis added). Play and art serve as transitional objects, connective tissue between self and others.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sexual addiction: Psychoanalytic concepts and the art of supervision, by V.D. Volkan, Oxfordshire, Phoenix Publishing House, 2021, 93 pp., $25.19 (paperback), ISBN-13 9781912691388

Drew Tillotson

Drew Tillotson

cogster@earthlink.net

Drew Tillotson  

“Sexual addiction” is one of those terms that has become heavily saturated, in the Bionian sense. It has become so pejoratively laden, so concrete with assumptions and grouped together with other kinds of addictive or compulsive behaviours, that it has been challenging for psychoanalysis to find space to move beyond a chemical addiction model in order to focus on what psychoanalysis does best: going underneath symptomatology to sources of suffering deeply concealed within the psyche. However, as Whitman-Raymond (2021) recently put it, “Addicts have had a rough go of it within the psychoanalytic community” (122). Our sexuality is our birthright. The use, meaning and frequency of sexual behaviour is as idiosyncratic for humans as are most behaviours. However, when sexual behaviour becomes a means of expressing a displacement of conflictual emotions, historical trauma, an avoidance of one’s internal life or of intimate human relationships, this places it squarely within the psychoanalytic endeavour in many ways dissimilar from the 12-Step model of abstinence.

In his latest book, Vamik Volkan, esteemed psychoanalyst, scholar and prolific author, takes up the phenomenon of sexually compulsive behaviour by describing the course of supervision over a period of three years of the case of “Judy”, who described herself as “a hunter of men for sex” (xii). As told, Volkan’s supervisee Dr Rowan came to Volkan seeking supervision on a difficult case while a candidate-in-training when, after two years of one session a week treatment, Dr Rowan felt no movement had occurred, “no workable transference development” (1), and Judy had been successful at keeping him at a distance. Volkan then agreed to supervise the case if Dr Rowan were successful at reframing the treatment to four times a week psychoanalysis on the couch, which Judy ultimately agreed to.

In his introduction, Volkan sets out four aims for writing the book, the main one being to describe and explicate the psychoanalytic process (both the analyst’s and his as supervisor) of the patient Judy, who Volkan states “was trying to solve her early life’s deprivations through remembering her deprived childhood self-image and wished for-love-object through actions, including a non-chemical addiction” (xiv). It appears that the thrust of Volkan’s volume is to contribute more to a psychoanalytic understanding of the symptoms and unconscious conflicts of non-chemical addictions, and in the case of Judy, what he terms “a sexual hunting addiction” (5).

responseSet.title: Why war? An Introduction
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Why war? An Introduction

Howard Levine

Howard Levine

hblevinemd@gmail.com
1318 Beacon Street, Suite #8, Brookline, MA 02446

Howard Levine  

I

At the root of “Why War?”, the Freud-Einstein Correspondence (Freud 1933), is the question of destructiveness and the essential nature of humankind. In his letter to Freud, Einstein asked: “Is there any way of delivering mankind from the menace of war?” (199).

He noted the all too present “craving for power”, social indifference, economic greed and self-interest of the ruling class (200) and wondered:

How is it possible for this small clique to bend the will of the majority, who stand to lose and suffer by a state of war, to the service of their ambitions? … An obvious answer to this question would seem to be that the majority, the ruling class at present, has the schools and press, usually the Church as well, under its thumb. This enables it to organise and sway the emotions of the masses and make its tool of them. Yet … How is it these devices succeed so well in rousing men to such wild enthusiasm, even to sacrifice their lives? (200-201)

Einstein’s conclusion was that:

Only one answer is possible. Because man has within him a lust for hatred and destruction … [and that] it is comparatively easy to call it into play and raise it to the power of a collective psychosis. (201)

Perhaps the most oft-quoted and remembered part of Freud’s reply was his reference to the innate destructiveness of the death instinct. However, his response was more complex and nuanced than simply this. After noting that the questions Einstein raised were the concern of statesmen and that he could only offer a psychological perspective rather than practical advice, Freud began by reminding Einstein that while

right and violence appear to us as antitheses. It can easily be shown … that the one has developed out of the other. (204)

That is, that rule of law was in its origins and at its root, violence deferred, displaced and reassigned to the community:

It is still violence, ready to be directed against any individual who resists it; it works by the same methods and follows the same purposes. The only real difference lies in the fact that what prevails is no longer the violence of an individual but that of a community. (205)

responseSet.title: The Case of J: Returning to the Office
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Case of J: Returning to the Office

Lena Theodorou Ehrlich

Lena Theodorou Ehrlich

Ann Arbor, MI, USA
lenaehrlich@drlenaehrlich.com

Lena Theodorou Ehrlich  

I thank the editor for inviting me back to serve as a discussant in this second of a two-part series of sessions on working during the pandemic, and I thank the analyst, Dr Y, for her largesse in once again sharing her work and permitting us to learn from it. As I did with the first set of sessions (Ehrlich 2020b), I received this second set as an invitation to share how this psychoanalytic process affected me and how my responses might be relevant in illuminating what is intrinsic in analysis: transference/counter-transference blind spots and enactments.

Initial Summary

In her introduction to this set of sessions, Dr Y summarises her experience of the analytic process in the months when she and J met on the phone as follows:

During the following 14 months voice was the only available source of emotional information. Initially, the hours on the phone had a strange eeriness, a hard to describe sense of loneliness. I was often aware of a sense of relief at the end of the hour. With the passing months the eeriness seemed to give way to frustration, which was at times overlooked as it seemed to have become a part of an uncomfortable “new normal” … We were often reminded of what we were missing: Loss of a sense of humanness that we could experience in the room, together. Interestingly, these challenges of remote work seemed to also disappear for extended periods of time only to abruptly appear at unexpected times, reminding both of us that this “new normal” was not the way we liked to work.

With this evocative description, Dr Y invites us into an analytic atmosphere dominated by a sense of derealization and dissociation. The passive tone in “the hours … had a strange eeriness” and “the eeriness … was at times overlooked” compellingly suggests that the analyst/patient dyad experienced this disturbing atmosphere as fated, inevitable, and justified by the loss of being “in the room, together”. Dr. Y.’s description of the “uncomfortable ‘new normal’” and “hard to describe sense of loneliness” gives the impression that these difficult-to-bear states of mind were viewed by the analyst as inherent in the setting and not considered as possible signals of a shared transferential/countertransferential experience of alienation and desolation. In my mind, this sense of fated, unavoidable loneliness resonates with the enactment in the transference/countertransference of an early formative overwhelming experience of deep helplessness and profound aloneness that included the imperative that it must not be acknowledged but be stoically endured until something better materialized in the future. The analyst appears to be suggesting

CONTACT Lena Theodorou Ehrlich lenaehrlich@drlenaehrlich.com

responseSet.title: Mirrors and Masquerades in Fleabag and Crazy Ex-Girlfriend
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mirrors and Masquerades in Fleabag and Crazy Ex-Girlfriend**

Catherine Humble

Catherine Humble

Catherine.humble@iopa.org.uk
London, UK

Catherine Humble  

In a wrenching scene towards the end of the first episode of Fleabag, the eponymous heroine (played by Phoebe Waller-Bridge) turns up at her dad’s door at two o’clock in the morning. Fleabag is upset. Like a stray cat, she looks like she’s tried every door down the street. Except this is her family home. Or rather - and it’s a big rather - her dad’s house.

“It’s nothing,” she says, when her father (played by Bill Paterson) opens the door, and promptly tells her, “It’s nearly 2 o’clock in the morning” (Waller-Bridge 2016a, 19:44-21:08).

Her dad guards the door, like she’s a salesperson. Fleabag hesitates, then blurts out:

Oh fuck it, I have a horrible feeling that I’m a greedy, perverted, selfish, apathetic, cynical, depraved, morally bankrupt woman who can’t even call herself a feminist. (Waller-Bridge 2016a, 19:44-21:08)

Beat.

You get all that from your mother. (Waller-Bridge 2016a, 19:44-21:08)

It could have been a funny response, if it wasn’t the middle of the night. If Fleabag wasn’t visibly distressed. If her mother wasn’t dead. In the first episode of the first series, the viewer learns that Fleabag’s mother - and her best friend - have recently passed away.

In a close-up shot we watch Fleabag’s face as it shifts from dejected yet hopeful, to shocked, to broken, before, in typical Fleabag style, she rallies around, laughs in the face of life’s shitshow.

“Good one.” (Waller-Bridge 2016a, 19:44-21:08)

The camera work in this series is as important as the dialogue. Her father’s look over the threshold of his doorstep is a failed look of recognition. Her dad cannot see what the viewer can - Fleabag’s visible pain - the close-up of her distorting, disturbed and disturbing face. And, a moment later, as Fleabag ascends the stairs, she does what the series is perhaps best known for - breaks the fourth wall. Abruptly looking the viewer in the eye,

CONTACT Catherine Humble Catherine.humble@iopa.org.uk

*This essay began as a seminar on TV and Psychoanalysis which I taught on the module Psychoanalysis and Other Disciplines as part of the UCL MSc in Theoretical Psychoanalytic Studies. The written paper was presented at the Maudsley Lecture in Psychoanalysis: The Arts and Psychoanalysis in 2021.

responseSet.title: Meeting the Sphinx
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Meeting the Sphinx11

Emma Lunbeck

Emma Lunbeck

Chicago, IL, USA
elunbeck@uchicago.edu

Emma Lunbeck  

Contemporary Kleinians, comparative psychoanalysis, Oedipus, dreams

Here I present a close re-reading and creative expansion of Richard Rusbridger's (2004) clinical work with a patient who reported a dream of a hybrid woman. Building on W. R. Bion's reading of the Oedipus myth and Melanie Klein's theory of the combined parent figure, and drawing on imagery ancient and modern, I re-interpret the patient's dream as an encounter with the Sphinx. Why does this enigmatic figure, threatening annihilation, emerge at this particular threshold in the patient's analysis? To explore this question, I return to Sophocles' Oedipus the King and offer a new translation of the exchange between Tiresias and Oedipus as they debate the king's famous victory over the monster. Where (and when) do we meet the Sphinx today? What form does the ancient monster take in modern life? In this paper, I ask us to consider what it means to meet the Sphinx—and how we might respond.

responseSet.title: Creative Engagement in Psychoanalytic Practice, by Henry Markman, London and New York, Routledge, 2022, 250 pp, $39.95, ISBN: 978-1-032-07723-9
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Creative Engagement in Psychoanalytic Practice, by Henry Markman, London and New York, Routledge, 2022, 250 pp, $39.95, ISBN: 978-1-032-07723-9

Robert Paul

Robert Paul

Stone Mountain, GA, USA
rpaul@emory.edu

Robert Paul  

The epigraph for the first chapter of Henry Markman’s creative and engaging book is a quotation from Freud’s “Recommendations to Physicians Practicing Psychoanalysis”, in which Freud makes it clear that the technique he recommends is only suited to his own individuality: “I do not venture to deny that a physician quite differently constituted might find himself driven to adopt a different attitude to his patients and to the task before him.” Accordingly, in Creative Engagement Markman explains step by step, in successive chapters, the elements of his own individual way of doing psychoanalysis. Along the way, he pays generous tribute to the many influences that have formed him as an analyst, including his own mentors and the writings of a wide range of analytic thinkers. He enlivens each chapter with one or more clinical vignettes to illustrate the concept under discussion.

Markman holds, as do many others, that the analytic relationship is the most important therapeutic factor in analysis. In this, he draws inspiration from the Ferenczi of the Clinical Diaries, Winnicott, the later Bion and numerous others, most interestingly the less well-known but influential Argentine analyst Pichon-Riviere, to whose concept of the vinculo, or “link”, Markman devotes a whole chapter. According to Pichon-Riviere, the analytic dyad, like any other human relationship, involves a dialectical circuit of communication between the participants. This circuit can be either alive and moving in a spiral in which new configurations can emerge; or it can be dead, empty, and repetitive. The task of the analyst is to remove impediments that block the free, vital circulation that will, if maintained, lead to a good therapeutic process. When this does not happen, the result is what Markman calls “non-process”. This may superficially sound like analysis, but nonetheless the most vulnerable and fearful aspects of the analysand’s psyche remain untouched and therefore unchanged. Markman’s book is devoted to showing how he monitors each member of the dyad’s reactions to the other and strives to ensure that optimum vitality and mutual engagement are maintained. While he presumably carries on this self-examination throughout the ordinary day-to-day analysis of defences and transferences, it is especially when the analysis hits an obstacle or goes stale that his distinctive method is most called for.

responseSet.title: Where the Id was, the Shared Ego must now be: The Acquisition of Symbolic Function, Language and Conscious
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Where the Id was, the Shared Ego must now be: The Acquisition of Symbolic Function, Language and Conscious**

Stefano Calamandrei

Stefano Calamandrei

stefano.calamandrei@spiweb.it
Firenze, Italy

Stefano Calamandrei  

Symbolic function; language; consciousness; psychoanalytical theory of symbol

The author contemplates the nature of symbolic function and reviews the most important theories on the topic, proposing developments of thought. While it has variously been argued that the symbols appearing in dreams and insanity are the same as those that occur in art, religion and folklore, symbolism has never been approached systematically. Freud came across symbolism during the 1910 to 1916 period, and he suggested it had an innate origin. Though psychoanalysis has analysed the nature of creativity, it hasn't outlined the process of developing and learning symbolism in the neonatal mind.

Recently, in infant observation and neuroscience, it has been argued that the newborn starts to learn symbolism from the moment he/she begins sharing a “hypercathectic” emotional state with the mother. Through maternal “hypercathexis” and identification, the newborn learns both the mechanism that creates symbols and the process of presentation, thus developing the working-through pathway that will form the preconscious, consciousness, and the sense of reality. Understanding the development of symbolism is greatly important for the treatment of severe pathologies and psychosis, which are likely caused by an altered development of symbolic functioning.

responseSet.title: Obituary for Edna O'Shaughnessy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Obituary for Edna O'Shaughnessy

Richard Rusbridger

Richard Rusbridger

rjr2700@gmail.com
27 Normanby Road, London NW10 1BU, UK

Richard Rusbridger  

Edna O’Shaughnessy, who has died aged 97, was a leading British psychoanalyst of both children and adults. Her papers are amongst the finest psychoanalytic writing. They have a rare combination of incisive clarity, imaginativeness and a great sensitivity to her patients’ deepest anxieties.

Tall, red-haired (known to all as “Red”), beautiful into old age and dignified in her bearing, O’Shaughnessy worked with patients and supervisees into her 90s. She was much loved and respected across the different theoretical groupings of the British Society. She worked in the tradition of Melanie Klein, a pioneer of child analysis who had added to Freud’s picture of the mind new discoveries about the earliest stages of psychic development.

O’Shaughnessy initially trained as a child psychotherapist, at the Tavistock Clinic, London, and retained a particular interest in children’s inner worlds. After the Tavistock, she trained as a psychoanalyst at the Institute of Psychoanalysis, London. She later became a training and supervising analyst, and a Distinguished Fellow, of the British Psychoanalytical Society. Her work is unified not so much by its subject matter, which is very diverse, but by her underlying preoccupations. These include the nature of psychic reality, of psychosis and of subjectivity.

Edna O’Shaughnessy was born Edna Rutovitz in 1924 in South Africa. Her parents’ families, both Jewish, had emigrated from Eastern Europe to escape the pogroms. The family of her father, Philip, whose own father was a rabbi, had come from Lithuania, while the family of her mother, Minnie, was from Bolton in England, having moved from Minsk in the late nineteenth century before moving again to South Africa in the early years of the twentieth. The family settled in Johannesburg, where her father owned a soap factory. Edna was the older of two children: her brother was born when she was 4. She excelled academically at school.

O’Shaughnessy had early contact with psychoanalysis: her father, who was well read, anti-religious and a socialist, had some Freud in his library. Her father died when she was 15, a crisis that led to her being separated from her mother and brother and going to live with the family of an aunt in Pretoria. Her mother remarried when O’Shaughnessy was 19.

responseSet.title: Distortions and Disjunctions of Temporalities in Times of Pandemic
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Distortions and Disjunctions of Temporalities in Times of Pandemic

Giuseppina Antinucci

Giuseppina Antinucci

London, UK

pina.antinucci@gmail.com
Via D'Ovidio 3, Milan, Italy

Giuseppina Antinucci  

Temporalities, pandemic, trauma, attention, Kairos, subject

Since the Covid pandemic required the implementation of restrictions, my patients have been concerned with their experience of time, which seemed distorted by the state of emergency impacting on our lives, and often elicited the feeling of inhabiting a dystopian world. There appear existential demands for subjective meaning, especially when the fear of death is so intense it appears uncontainable. The state of uncertainty impacts upon the sense of the future, and hence on desire and hope: affect states that emerge primarily from the internal world. Within the framework of Western thought, time is linear (Cronos), cyclical and recursive (Aion) or fugitively punctual (Kairos). These figures of time implicate the interaction between the internal and external world within a first-person account. The paper focuses on Kairos - that critical moment where the subject's sense of reality through attention serves self-protective functions and leads to action. Kairos is also the temporality of trauma. Vignettes from two patients, of different ages and in different phases of analyses, illustrate the subjective vicissitudes of Kairos, depending on the state of the self, ego, and biographical inscriptions in a wider human chronology. Freud's equation: perception=attention=time captures the psychic work and significance of the temporality of Kairos.

responseSet.title: Response to: “On Translating Case Material into a Language Different from the Original”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to: “On Translating Case Material into a Language Different from the Original”

Abbot Bronstein PHD

Abbot Bronstein

abbot.bronstein@gmail.com

Abbot Bronstein  

Dr Matthias von der Tann,

I am grateful for the close reading done on the case of Yune. There are too many points to attempt to address them all, and I will only take up a few. As the editor of the AAW section there is always a balance that must be struck between what can be discussed within a limited space and how the analytic material is presented. The hope is always to have genuine, accessible clinical material presented with very little theory put forth on the part of the presenter along with the clinical material. Often the analyst wishes to explain what they were intending to do or why. But if the case material and discussions stimulate the kind of response this letter describes, I feel we have done our task.

In the case of Yune, we had some very specific issues that needed to be examined and discussed. The material was from a Korean analyst, with a Korean patient. The language of the analysis was Korean. I and the discussants, maybe because we were too close to the material, thought this was clear to any reader. The language of the analysis is of course very important. Doing analysis in one's original spoken language must, for both analyst and patient, be considered carefully, with nuances of language often being missed.

In this case that was in part the task of the translation of this material into English. This went through multiple iterations and took well over a year. Discussions about meanings in translations, the complications of nuance, the context that altered the meanings, etc., even the gender implications of certain words and actions, were all discussed with the analyst, my editorial readers and an outside consultant who knew the case and the analyst very well. This was an arduous task, which we hope paid off in terms of the clarity of the case material. Interactions with her father, analyst, husband, and her work all were subject to our questions and discussions with Dr Rho. The translation was not, nor ever is in the IJP, done via a computer program. Here the analyst, a translator and the editorial group all had an input.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud, Sullivan, Mitchell, Bion and the Multiple Voices of International Psychoanalysis by Marco Conci, International Psychoanalytic Books, New York, 2019, 735 pp., $35.00 (paperback), ISBN 9781949093346

Giovanni Foresti

Giovanni Foresti

battista.foresti@gmail.com

Giovanni Foresti  

The conspicuous yet agile book published by Marco Conci in 2019 - Freud, Sullivan, Mitchell, Bion and the Multiple Voices of International Psychoanalysis - is an original and very useful volume, which will certainly become an essential companion for scholars and students alike. It is “conspicuous” because it collects a wide array of rigorous and thoughtful research into 538 various relevant fields. And “agile” because the author and contributors help the reader feel oriented and able to easily read its rather formidable 700 pages.

Born on the border of Italy and Austria, Marco is a psychiatrist and psychoanalyst who was trained in various institutes and cities and now lives and works mostly in Germany, Munich, but also in Trento, his home town. He is a member of both the Italian Psychoanalytic Society and the DPG, the Deutsche Psychoanalytische Gesellschaft. His book is therefore the mature result of an uncommonly wide array of clinical experiences and well digested theoretical traditions. The approach proposed in the central part of the book (278-331) can be summarized by the formula “new comparative psychoanalysis”, and the perspective that these pages advocate is concisely described in the title of the penultimate part of the book: “international psychoanalysis” and “international identity”.

As a researcher with a solid historical methodology, Conci compares the documents he studies with the contexts, the personalities and the human groups they come from and belong to. As the reading proceeds, one can clearly see that what is “new” in his perspective comes from his habit of dealing with the literature of the present day with the same attention and cautiousness that historians devote to archives. The histories that are read here are not the type of hagiographic and apologetic narratives that Gramsci named “fetishistic history” (because the research is historicist and sees “the chicken in the fertilized egg”). In these pages, the past is an unforeseeable archive, full of interesting events that have remained unknown and are relevant because their deferred effects lie not behind, but within us.

responseSet.title: Repetition and Breakdown: Freud, Winnicott, and Death
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Repetition and Breakdown: Freud, Winnicott, and Death

Johannes Picht

Johannes Picht

Schliengen, Germany
johannes.picht@web.de

Johannes Picht  

Representational structure; trauma; passage à l'acte

Freud’s “repetition compulsion” and Winnicott’s “breakdown” are two divergent concepts that both arise out of an effort to think the unthinkable of death and annihilation. The relationship between these concepts will be examined in terms of their clinical, psychoanalytically theoretical, and philosophical aspects. Freud spoke of the “fright” of trauma, which threatens the functioning of the psychic apparatus, but then he devised a representational conception of death, leading to the hypothesis of the death drive. Winnicott rejected the latter yet he made direct reference to Freud in his theory of trauma. A clinical vignette will show how a represented element (repetition) and an unrepresented element (breakdown) interact in repetition compulsion, leading to the thesis that the compulsory nature of repetition compulsion originates in the threat of breakdown, which also involves the environment (the analyst). This leads to further reflections on the different and yet mutually expository paths that Freud and Winnicott took to make death and annihilation available to thought. An investigation into the temporal structures of repetition and breakdown shows that breakdown involves a breakdown of represented time and therefore cannot be assigned to any point in time. Lastly, on the basis of clinical material, the question is raised as to how an action that represents breakdown can come about: witnesses who interpret an event as an intentional action play a decisive role here. The paper concludes with comments on the implications for treatment.

responseSet.title: The Murder of the Dead Father: The Shoah and Contemporary Antisemitism
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Murder of the Dead Father: The Shoah and Contemporary Antisemitism

Rosine Perelberg

Rosine Perelberg

rperelberg@perelberg.com
London, UK

Rosine Perelberg  

“banality of evil”; Murdered father; Dead father

It is suggested in this paper that in the Shoah one is confronted with the abolition of the Law of the Dead Father and the re-establishing of the tyranny of the narcissistic father. In the extermination of the Jews of Europe in the Shoah, the aim was the destruction of the rules of genealogy and filiation to both mother and father that establish the social and give rise to personhood and are at the core of the oedipal structure. The rule of absolute power - the destruction of any sense of maternal care and paternal rules - leads ultimately to the creation of the abject.

Freud distinguished between two different types of obstacles to psychoanalytic treatment that are expressions of the death drive. The first is bound and is related to the superego; it is connected to the negative therapeutic reaction, masochism, and the unconscious sense of guilt. The other manifestation of the death drive is unbound and diffuse. If the first is understandable, the second, he suggests, escapes any understanding. The paper makes use of this distinction to examine Hannah Arendt’s notion of the banality of evil.

responseSet.title: Madeleine and Willy Baranger’s Contribution to Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Madeleine and Willy Baranger’s Contribution to Psychoanalysis

Jorge Maldonado

Jorge Maldonado

jorgeluismaldonado12@gmail.com
3085, 5B, Buenos Aires CABA, Argentina

Jorge Maldonado  

Psychoanalytic field, bastion, resignification

This paper provides a summary of the careers of the psychoanalysts Madeleine and Willy Baranger and the context in which their ideas developed. It will outline their training contributions in Latin American psychoanalytic associations, which culminated in the foundation of the Uruguayan Psychoanalytic Association. The theory of the bipersonal field, later known as the intersubjective field, will be described. This was conceptualized to study the analytic situation when problems of stagnation arise in the analytic process and focuses on the common, unique unconscious phantasy created between analyst and analysand. The concept of the “bastion” and the notion of the “second look” directed toward the intersubjective field will be described as well as the differences between the theory of the intersubjective field and the concept of enactment. The repetition of the analyst will be considered, in particular when the analyst only uses one theory. The distinction between Klein’s early Oedipus and the Freudian Oedipus complex, which the Barangers considered to be two different concepts, will be explored. The essential points of Madeleine Baranger’s article on “bad faith” will be presented. Finally, the concept of resignification and the use of theoretical complementarity will be highlighted.

responseSet.title: On The logic of Unconscious Objects
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On The logic of Unconscious Objects**

Amit Saad

Amit Saad

Tel-Aviv, Israel
Shoshana Persits St 3, 61394

Tel Aviv, Israel

Amit Saad  

Unconscious, logic, object-relation

The aim of this paper is to distinguish between conscious and unconscious conceptions of objects. I will argue that we consciously ascribe different properties to the same object based on their coexistence in time and space. For example, the colour and flavour of an apple are ascribed to the same object as they relate to the same spatiotemporal location. However, since there is no reference to time in unconscious thinking, we cannot ascribe properties unconsciously to objects based on spatiotemporality. Instead, I hypothesize, properties are unconsciously ascribed to the same object based on the drive they satisfy. This hypothesis accounts for substantial unconscious processes such as condensation, displacement, splitting and certain linguistic features of unconscious thinking. Moreover, it enables the identification of a type of suppression of unconscious content—logical suppression—used to classify levels of personality organization. The clinical merit of this hypothesis will be presented through various case reports.

responseSet.title: The Infantile: More Than Hilflosigkeit?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Infantile: More Than Hilflosigkeit?

Adela Abella

Adela Abella

adela.abella@bluewin.ch
Switzerland
217 rte. d'Annecy, 1257 Croix de Rozon, CH

Adela Abella  

To the Editor:

The very rich and varied lectures and panels of the 52nd IPA Congress 2021 that I was able to attend share what I see as a historically determined but partial and clinically limitative concept of the infantile. This view of the infantile is heir to the Freudian notion of Hilflosigkeit and highlights its “negative” aspects. The lexical field of its English translation, helplessness, as well as the corresponding terms in other languages (desamparo in Spanish, état de détresse/desaide in French) encompass notions such as dependence, immaturity, abandonment, vulnerability, loneliness, fragility, etc.

An important distinction has often been made between the psychoanalytic infantile and the infant. The infantile is understood as a resurgence/construction of the modalities of early inscription of experience that are construed in the transference. According to the authors, this infantile is conceived in terms of a supposedly primordial and unknowable sexuality; in terms of basic needs of security and protection; of traumatic sequelae linked to the ontogenetic helplessness of the human being; of concrete and non-symbolized modes of thought, etc. The aim of an analysis would be precisely the transformation of the infantile which remains unrevealed in the adult, in the adolescent and in the child. From this point of view, the infantile maintains a contingent link with the infant/ the child; the analyst elaborates hypotheses on the beginnings of mental functioning drawing on the transference-countertransference. A much-debated subject is the contribution of infants’ observation to the construction of such a psychoanalytic infantile. In fact, opinions oscillate between those who regard it as accessory and fundamentally not significant, and those who consider that, at least, there should be no flagrant contradiction between what is analytically construed and what is observed.

Psychoanalysis evolves within a given society and within a language with its meanings, its over-understandings and its emotional tone that imperceptibly infiltrate the technical terms in the different fields. In Spanish we find the following synonyms of infantile: pueril, aniñado, candoroso, ingenuo, inocente, cándido, simple. In French: puéril, immature, modeste, bébête, élémentaire, facile. In English: typical of a small child (and therefore not suitable for adults or older children), childish. These terms share certain derogatory, disparaging and inglorious connotations. In a similar spirit, in the psychoanalytic literature, the infantile is linked to the archaic, the primitive, the pregenital, the poorly mentalized, the pathological, the psychotic, etc. Implicit in these connections is the idea of a hierarchy in the stages of life in which the adult is set as the unit of measurement to

CONTACT Adela Abella adela.abella@bluewin.ch 217 rte. d'Annecy, 1257 Croix de Rozon, CH.

responseSet.documentInfoXML: 2022 103 1 236-240 A Psychoanalytic Perspective On Reading Literature: Reading The Reader by Merav Roth, New York, Routledge, 2020, 336 pp., $46.95, ISBN 978-1-138-39131-4 Benjamin H. Ogden bogden80@gmail.com
responseSet.title: A Psychoanalytic Perspective On Reading Literature: Reading The Reader by Merav Roth, New York, Routledge, 2020, 336 pp., $46.95, ISBN 978-1-138-39131-4
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Psychoanalytic Perspective On Reading Literature: Reading The Reader by Merav Roth, New York, Routledge, 2020, 336 pp., $46.95, ISBN 978-1-138-39131-4

Benjamin Ogden

Benjamin Ogden

bogden80@gmail.com

Benjamin Ogden  

It is difficult to write persuasively and intelligently about reading itself. Reading, like dreaming, is a subjective experience that leaves no imprint. As vivid and complex as a reading experience may be, as soon as it is over there is no solid evidence left behind that it ever happened. So to write about what it is like to read literature, or what happens in our minds while we read literature, requires that we write about something that has never had a physical form, and only ever existed as a singular, unreproducible event (no two reading experiences are identical). Texts and lives are material things whose reality can be attested to by multiple parties, whereas reading experiences are dreams we have had. It is no wonder that so much more has been written about literary texts and the lives of their authors than has been written about either the phenomenology or the psychology of reading literature (although the 1960s did spawn a movement of reader-response theory, made famous by Stanley Fish’s incomparable reading of Milton in Surprised by Sin: The Reader in Paradise Lost).

In A Psychoanalytic Perspective on Reading Literature: Reading the Reader, Merav Roth, training analyst and chair of the psychotherapy programme at the Sackler School of Medicine in Tel Aviv, Israel, has found an inspired way around the difficulty described above. Rather than focusing on her own affective responses to reading literature, she turns her attention to 18 works of literature in which characters read:

In each of these works the reading experiences of their literary heroes appear directly and indirectly, openly and covertly. It has to be emphasized that I will be exploring the reading experiences of the various characters in these literary works rather than analyzing my reading experience as the reader, though that aspect is not, of course, entirely absent. (1)

In choosing to examine the reading experiences of characters from literature, Roth is able to study textual remnants of reading experiences: the subjectivity of the reading experience fossilized in literary texts.

We are Looking Deeper than Freud … On the Departure from the Primacy of the Sexual in Berlin and London between 1920 and 1925*1*1

Ulrike May

Ulrike May

may.tolzmann@t-online.de
Deutschland
Taunusstr.12, 12161 Berlin, Germany

Ulrike May  

Between 1920 and 1925 a shift occurred in psychoanalytic theory that related primarily to the status of aggression. The author shows which authors in Berlin, Amsterdam and London participated in this change and to what extent they prepared the ground for the reception of Melanie Klein, who developed in her analyses of children a technique that applied pre-eminently to the perception and interpretation of aggression. The way in which Freud distanced himself from this departure from the primacy of the sexual is described, as well as the waning importance of his perspective following the establishment of the Institutes in Berlin and London.

responseSet.title: Marie Eglé Laufer (1925-2021)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Marie Eglé Laufer (1925-2021)

Catalina Bronstein

Catalina Bronstein

catalinabronstein@gmail.com
London, UK

Catalina Bronstein  

I would like to share my sadness about the death of Eglé Laufer and to give a brief account of some of her main contributions. I consider myself fortunate to have been able to enjoy her friendship as well as to work with her and learn from her.

Eglé Laufer’s contribution to psychoanalysis has been profound and highly enriching. Some of her ideas were jointly discussed and developed together with her husband Moses Laufer (known as Moe), and within the context of working at the Brent Consultation Centre (now the Brent Centre for Young People) in London. Others arose just from her own experience and provided a different view from the notions that were already in place. Eglé brought a different perspective on the relationship to the body in adolescence and, in particular, to the relationship to the body in the adolescent girl. She also paid special attention to the infantile precursors of adolescent pathology. Her contributions to the understanding of adolescents’ relationship to their body, which she describes in terms of the reality of the “sexual” body, was of great help in understanding the reasons why adolescents frequently attack their bodies and/or try to transform them.

Eglé described her development in a paper published in 2009 in Psychoanalytic Enquiry (M. E. Laufer 2009). She was born in Vienna in 1925 at a time when many young intellectuals, like her parents, were inspired by the Russian revolution and became involved in left-wing politics.1 Her father was a Hungarian economist called Imre Vajda who had fled from Hungary following the failed 1919 revolution under threat of a death sentence. After the liberation of Hungary from fascism he became one of the leaders of the left wing Social Democratic Party.

Eglé's mother Sigrid was Austrian. She studied at Vienna University and became a qualified doctor, later working as a child psychiatrist. They lived in Vienna and Eglé attended the nursery school founded by Maria Montessori. Then, in the early 1930s, Eglé’s parents went through a painful divorce. Shortly after, Eglé’s mother married an Austrian aircraft engineer called Edwin Pribram, and Eglé did not see her biological father again until 1958 when she met him briefly in Brussels. Her father was sentenced to death by Stalin and was in his jail cell awaiting execution when Stalin died. Shortly after, he was released and then became a respectable academic professor of economics in Budapest. Eglé’s stepfather, Edwin Pribram, was the son of a Viennese professor of history who was a friend of Freud and part of the same Jewish intellectual circle in Vienna.

L’Ora della nascita. Psicoanalisi del sublime e arte contemporanea*, by Giuseppe Civitarese, Milan, Jaka Book, 2020, €20, San Giuliano Milanese (MI), Review by Monica Bomba

Monica Bomba

Monica Bomba

monica.bomba@gmail.com
Milan, Italy
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-4555-8636

Monica Bomba  

L’Ora della nascita. Psicoanalisi del sublime e arte contemporanea by Giuseppe Civitarese offers an original revision of the concept of sublimation. Starting with the uncanny of Freud and passing through the abjection of Julia Kristeva, the author invites us to devise and forge a new conceptual tool, not only illustrating it, but also taking the reader into the direct, living experience of it in the art of the sublime. The author does not “explain” the manifest content of the relationship between the art of the sublime and psychoanalysis; he is *The Hour of Birth: Psychoanalysis of the Sublime and Contemporary Art. Translation by Adam Elgar.420

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Francis Grier

Francis Grier

fjrgrier@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3757-852X

Francis Grier  

War is the context for this issue of the International Journal. The world has been stunned by the Russian invasion of Ukraine. I am writing this editorial in mid-April, but by the time this issue is published in June I would expect, sadly, that hostilities will still be ongoing.

I have asked Oleksandra Mirza and Igor Romanov of the Ukrainian psychoanalytic IPA study group to contribute a Letter From Ukraine, which you will find in this issue, in which they depict and reflect upon the catastrophe which has befallen them. Renate Kohlheimer, from the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society, has also written a Letter from Vienna. The original purpose was for her to speak to the international psychoanalytic community regarding the European Psychoanalytic Federation’s forthcoming July conference. Vienna, however, has found itself caught up in the frontline of rushing to the aid of Ukrainians since Austria is a neighbour of Ukraine, and that has meant Austrian psychoanalysts have been particularly involved in helping Ukrainian psychoanalysts and their families, many of whom have sought sanctuary in Vienna - so the war also figures in Renate’s Letter.

The Education Section committee of the International Journal has for some time been planning to publish a number of articles on the subject of war, inspired by Freud’s and Einstein’s correspondence on the subject. It had not been anticipated that a major European war would erupt imminently, though vicious wars have been waged alongside political violence without cease in various parts of the world in recent years - Syria, Ethiopia, Myanmar, Chechnya, Sudan, Yemen immediately spring to mind. The difference is that none of these conflicts are in psychoanalytic centres, so most of the psychoanalytic world has been protected by their geographical distance from suffering the direct impact of war. Ukraine, by contrast, has a very active psychoanalytic community, well in touch with many psychoanalysts worldwide, and particularly with the IPA.

Russia also has its own highly engaged society of psychoanalysts, who have been thrown into utter disarray by the terrors which their country is perpetrating in their name. It is politically impossible for the Russian analysts to be able to make any kind of formal communication, quite apart from the fact that their work has also been devastated by the exigencies of the current situation. They are also in our thoughts.

responseSet.title: The Case of J: Returning to the Office
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Case of J: Returning to the Office

Francis Grier

Francis Grier

http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3757-852X
London
fjrgrier@gmail.com

Francis Grier  

29 October 2021

It’s a privilege to be asked to comment again on this very interesting work between analyst and patient, now that they have resumed face-to-face psychoanalysis after the many months of telephone analysis caused by Covid. It’s also extremely interesting.

To engage in this task, I decided initially against looking at the previous sessions and the commentaries on it. Indeed, I tried to go further and “forget” - in the spirit of Bion’s injunctions - any previous thoughts I had entertained. Paradoxically, this discipline also pertains to the “fact” of being informed that the analytic couple have reconvened face-to-face. That is obviously such a major factor, and it is what has motivated the editor to ask the same team of analytic commentators to comment on the present sessions. But this raises a dilemma, which we often meet in analytic work in various variations. The danger is that we will see everything in this particular light, which would certainly go against Bion’s injunction, because it would come under the heading of “memory”. On the other hand, how foolish would it be not to take this into account! So, perhaps artificially, I have oscillated between both perspectives, remembering and “forgetting”.

The dilemma continues in a subtle form within the sessions themselves, because, naturally, both patient and analyst referred to the break. And it must be right to take their meaning literally. However, only to hear these words referring concretely to the Covid break would be to lose the possibility that either party may, through their words, be speaking symbolically, which is of course an everyday occurrence in analysis as we are all trained to realise - though how easy is it to assume that “a cigar is just a cigar” when it might be more importantly a symbolic expression of something else.

My sense is that there is one particular theme, and then thread, of great importance throughout the three sessions. It is also present, through its absence, in the general introduction the analyst has thoughtfully provided. When I read this first, I was immediately struck by the thoughtful and rational tone of the account. The analyst tells us that what was upsetting was the loss of humanness from not being in the room together, and for the most part things are better or even good, particularly in terms of J’s marriage and family possibilities, and his work. Where, I wondered, was anything irrational, unsettling, let alone anything provocative of real fear or dread?

responseSet.title: A Psychological Analysis of the Causes of War
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Psychological Analysis of the Causes of War11

Roger Money-Kyrle

Roger Money-Kyrle

Roger Money-Kyrle  

Many different theories of the origin of war have been suggested. Quite possibly they may all be true. Each of them may help to explain some wars; some of them may help to explain all wars.

We can divide these theories into two main types: those which deal with what medical science would call precipitating causes and those which deal with predisposing or constitutional causes. Take, for instance, the analogy of a cold. Here exposure to infection, or to the weather, are precipitating causes. But these alone would not be enough to produce a cold without some constitutional disposition - a weak chest, enlarged tonsils, or whatever it may be.

So it is, I believe, with the social disease of war. Many things, sometimes quite opposite things, may be precipitating causes: such as political assassinations, or having an army so big that it annoys your neighbours or so small that they are tempted to ignore you. These may be compared with going out in the rain or forgetting an overcoat. There remains, however, the constitutional disposition. People who dislike pacifism often say that it is human nature to fight and that human nature can't be changed. How far is this true?

Unconscious Aggression

The psycho-analytical school of psychology, founded by Freud, has recently made some important discoveries about the destructive impulses in man. And Dr Edward Glover, in particular, has applied them to a study of the causes of war. That man in a state of nature is an aggressive animal was always fairly obvious. It was also clear that the civilized man could return to savagery much too easily for his own or other people's safety. But what was not known was that the destructive impulses, which break out in war, are always present in our unconscious minds. We know nothing about them because they are repressed [or split-off]; but they are there and ready to break out.

If this is admitted, we begin to understand one of the reasons why wars so often happen, even when everyone seems to be trying to prevent them. We are like people who go about without knowing that their pockets are full of dynamite. The more we realize this, the more likely we shall be to take all possible precautions.

responseSet.title: The Phylogenetic Argument in Freud’s Metapsychology of Anxiety
responseSet.authorMast: Francisco Pizarro Obaid and  Rodrigo De la Fabián
responseSet.authorCitation: Obaid, F. P. & De la Fabián, R.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Phylogenetic Argument in Freud’s Metapsychology of Anxiety

Francisco Obaid and Rodrigo De la Fabián

Rodrigo De la Fabián

francisco.pizarro@udp.cl
Santiago, Chile

Rodrigo De la Fabián  

Freud; phylogenesis; anxiety

Freud’s theorising about anxiety has traditionally been based on its nosographical categories (anxiety neurosis, anxiety hysteria) or on the relationship between anxiety and repression (first and second theories of anxiety). While these types of approach have made it possible to identify some milestones in the development of the concept of anxiety, they have also obscured the relevance that Freud attributed to the phylogenetic argument. This article reviews the historical and conceptual context of Freud’s main evolutionary references (Lamarck, Darwin, Haeckel, Weismann), and then analyses their presence and function in Freud’s work, especially in his conception of anxiety. The article shows that while some of the evolutionary references became obsolete towards the end of the 19th century, they played such an important role in Freud’s work that he never ceased to defend them. With respect to anxiety, though Freud did not make fully public his metapsychological hypothesis about the phylogenetic rationale, this perspective permeates his writing and is fundamental to his development of the concepts of neurotic anxiety/realistic anxiety and automatic anxiety/signal anxiety.

responseSet.title: Cézanne and the post-Bionian field: an exploration and a meditation, by Robert Snell, London and New York, Routledge, 2021, USD 39.95, ISBN: 978-0-367-64547-2
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Cézanne and the post-Bionian field: an exploration and a meditation, by Robert Snell, London and New York, Routledge, 2021, USD 39.95, ISBN: 978-0-367-64547-2

Katherine Nahum

Katherine Nahum

khnahum@gmail.com

Katherine Nahum  

Dr Robert Snell meditates upon Paul Cézanne’s relationship to post-Bionian field theory in this short, personal book. It is packed as well with examples of apparent correspondence between Cézanne’s recorded statements and those of later creative minds including Merleau-Ponty, the art historian T.J. Clark, Wilfred Bion and other psychoanalysts of various theoretical stripe. It is a hybrid work blending a psychoanalytic theory with diverse commentary by philosophers, psychoanalysts, art historians and poets. The beauty of Snell’s homage is that the artist’s “symptoms” are of modest interest.

As a historian of modern European art I can only understand the book as a complex homage to a great artist, because Snell’s central idea that Cézanne anticipated the tenets of post-Bionian field theory does not hold up. There is no reason to assume that Cézanne was interested in the nature of human relationships or those relationships, as Snell describes, within a broad field that included objects. Although Snell wants to engage a “conversation” between Cézanne and field theorists, the artist’s “field” was narrow, solipsistic. In the later years it comprised the motif - what he found visually and viscerally captivating in nature, and how he might transpose what he saw to his canvas by means of a style that held risk - because it was discovered in the moment, and might not be discovered at all.

Cézanne’s artistic followers misunderstood his work, yet their creative paintings led to important twentieth-century movements and styles that we admire. Later commentary on the artist is fascinating, but neither those words nor his own words “echo” (xii) any psychoanalytic theory, if only because we can trust neither the reporters nor Cézanne himself, whose ideas were often changing.

Inspired by the Cézanne “Portraits” exhibition in London, Snell follows their “schematic four-part outline of Cézanne’s journey” (xix-xx). He will:

liken Cézanne’s career to that of a psychoanalyst who set out as a humanistic, person-centred learner, then with the help of an analyst/supervisor/mentor (the Impressionist painter Camille Pissarro, in Cézanne’s case), finds his way deeper into himself and into contemporary theory and practice, and then, like Bion, spends the rest of his life confronting the unrelenting difficulties, the uncertainties, mysteries, and indeterminacies inherent to his art, the doubts they engender in him, and what he might learn from them. It is a career that might offer food for thought for therapeutic education. (xx)

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis Enters the Political Fray: The op-ed Articles and Journal Blogs of Peter Wolson by Peter Wolson, Queens, NY, International Psychoanalytic Books, 2019; 233pp., $30, ISBN: 978-1-949093-14-8

Richard Reichbart

Richard Reichbart

reichbart@earthlink.net
60 West Ridgewood Ave., Ridgewood, N.J., 07450, U.S.A.
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-9034-7256

Richard Reichbart  

This book is a collection of 25 popular political essays (in the nature of op-ed articles and blogs) written by the California psychoanalyst Peter Wolson that appeared in select American newspapers. Twelve of them are from the Huffington Post, eight from the Los Angeles Times and a few from other publications, such as Counter Punch, International Psychoanalysis and Thomson Reuters (as well as one never before published). They begin with an early one (from 1972) about Thomas Eagleton, the erstwhile Vice Presidential candidate whom George McGovern chose as a running mate and who was then summarily dropped from the Democratic ticket when the media discovered he had undergone a course of ECT as a youth. And after a break of almost 30 years the essays continue on a variety of subjects, ending in a slew that touch on the years of George Bush, Hillary Clinton and Donald Trump. The last one appeared in 2017 and the book was published in 2019 so that the strange events that have taken place in the USA in the first impeachment of President Trump, the 2020 election and what happened afterward do not figure, although some of these events were anticipated by Wolson.

The essays are accompanied by text before and afterward in which Wolson explains about the background, process and goals of each essay. This background sometimes makes it rather difficult to recognize where the article ends and where the hindsight begins. Because they are so diverse (ranging from Barack Obama’s candidacy to the awful torture that America administered at the Abu Ghraib prison in Iraq to the Penn State coach Joe Paterno’s failure to prevent his assistant Jerry Sandusky from raping young men) I only discuss some of the op-ed pieces here.

Wolson’s basic plea to US psychoanalysts is that we not shy away from speaking out to the public about political matters and that we have a moral duty to do so. He says:

Joining the media dialogue about the “breaking news of the day” could enrich and enhance American public’s understanding of unconscious political motivation while making psychoanalysis more accessible and user-friendly … Ultimately, it might even help resolve the diverse and enduring political conflicts that have plagued humanity. (202)

responseSet.title: Correction
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Correction

Article title: The case of J: Returning to the office

Authors: Anonymous

Journal: The International Journal of Psychoanalysis

Bibliometrics: Volume 103, Number 3, pages 496 - 498

DOI: http://dx.doi.org/10.1080/00207578.2022.2068769

When this article was originally published, the following text was missing from the article. This has now been updated in the online version.

June 01, 2021 (Tuesday)

First session after returning to the office-

J missed the first two weeks of sessions after I reopened my office. He was out visiting his family during this time. He arrived for his session on time on Tuesday since Monday was a public holiday. I greeted him: “Good afternoon J.” He replied looking at me: “Good afternoon Dr. Y.”

We walked to the office, with me walking behind him. He laid down on the couch, after leaving his keys and cellphone on the other chair as if a year and a half had not elapsed since he was last in this room. I sat down on my chair behind the couch.)

responseSet.title: Purgatorio by Dante Alighieri, Translated from the Italian and with an Introduction by D. M. Black, Preface by Robert Pogue Harrison, New York, NY, Review of Books, 2021, 459 pp., $19.95. ISBN 978-1-68137-605-9
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Purgatorio by Dante Alighieri, Translated from the Italian and with an Introduction by D. M. Black, Preface by Robert Pogue Harrison,* New York, NY, Review of Books, 2021, 459 pp., $19.95. ISBN 978-1-68137-605-9

Hattie Myers

Hattie Myers

Hatbmyers@gmail.com

Hattie Myers  

Walter Benjamin (2019) wrote, “A translation, instead of resembling the meaning of the original, must lovingly and in detail incorporate the original’s mode of signification, thus making the original and the translation recognizable as fragments of a greater language” (Illuminations, 21). As a poet, a psychoanalyst and a student of religion, this is precisely what D. M. Black has accomplished in this stunning new translation of Dante’s “Purgatorio”.

Both Dante and Freud strove to transform the human soul and the collective heart of humankind. For 700 years, serious readers of Dante have been plunged into a warren of complexity and depth, and more than a few have devoted entire lives trying to get as close as possible - poetically, critically and/or spiritually - to Dante’s journey. For over 100 years serious practitioners of psychoanalysis have similarly dived in head first, devoting their lives to plumbing the theoretical complexities of what the late Janet Malcolm referred to as our “impossible profession”. Coming to the Divine Comedy in the middle of my professional life as an analyst, with no background in Italian, I sensed, as any psychoanalyst might, the “fragments of a greater language”. Black’s translation and commentary makes legible these profound interconnections and illuminates the inner workings of both Freud’s and Dante’s art.

Black’s decision to begin his translation in the centre of Dante’s poem is significant. From Purgatory, Black is positioned to give his readers a bird’s eye view of the entire Christian afterlife, a synthetic sweep of Dante’s unusual journey. Black’s introduction at the beginning, his commentary at the end and the wealth of footnotes that gloss his translation throughout provide all the scaffolding a first-time reader will require to find their bearings. And for readers already familiar with Dante, the duet of an exquisite translation accompanied by psychoanalytically attuned notes is a welcome occasion for revisiting this epic poem.

responseSet.title: Theories and Practices of Psychoanalysis in Central Europe Immediately after World War II
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Theories and Practices of Psychoanalysis in Central Europe Immediately after World War II

Agnieszka Sobolewska

Agnieszka Sobolewska

agnieszka.sobolewska2021@gmail.com
Prague, Czech Republic
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-2119-7215

Krakowskie Przedmiescie 26/28, 00-927 Warsaw, Poland

Agnieszka Sobolewska  

History of psychoanalysis; Freudian theory; transcultural psychoanalysis; historical research; identity; war

Shortly after World War II, psychoanalytic societies in Central Europe were gradually resuming their pre-war activities. Starting in 1945, Austria, Hungary, Czechoslovakia and Poland saw the revival of their psychoanalytic circles which subsequently engaged in psychoanalytic knowledge transmission, especially in the face of the Communist state institutions’ growing disapproval of psychoanalysis. This article traces the history of the psychoanalytic movement’s rebirth in Central Europe. The author discusses the activities of Viennese, Budapestian, Praguian and Varsovian circles post-1945 in order to examine the practices of collective thinking and identify diverse models of the transmission of Freudianism. The attempt to explore the complex mechanisms of psychoanalytic knowledge dissemination in the immediate post-war period, both in its theoretical and practical dimensions, can contribute to a more profound understanding of the history of psychoanalysis in Central Europe after 1945. It also points to the significance of a more inquisitive approach to the internal dynamics of these intellectual circles which were forced to develop outside of state academic institutions due to socio-political reasons.

responseSet.title: Obituary of Antoine Corel MD (23 May 1935-14 July 2022)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Obituary of Antoine Corel MD (23 May 1935-14 July 2022)

We are sad to report that Antoine Corel, a former Member of the Board of our Journal, on which he served for more than 15 years, has died of Covid in Paris. He was especially valued on the Board for his deep knowledge of Freud (and post-Freudians), and he could be counted on to spot any errors. Born in Argentina, he began his career as a medical doctor in Lanari Institute of Clinical Research. As a cinema critic for Diario la Prensa, he wrote 1000 reviews signed A.C. He was a member of the International Jury of Critics with Jean Baroncelli (France) and Alexander Mackendrick (England) for the International Mar del Plata Festival (1968), and as an example of his activities, he interviewed Passolini and Callas about Medea (1970).

His training analyst in Argentine Psychoanalytic Association (IPA) was Rebeca Grinberg. He wrote an essay on construction in Freud’s work, hailed by Horacio Etchegoyen as a turning point at a time when this concept was rarely used. His approach was that “the patient’s history is constructed in the psychoanalytic process” (written in 1976, the year of his emigration from Argentina to France with his wife Haydee Faimberg and two children Claudina and Eduardo). Dr Corel and Dr Faimberg became prominent Members of the Société Psychanalytique de Paris (IPA).

Antoine Corel’s essays presented at IPA congresses - where he made a significant contribution to our understanding of the interface between film and psychoanalysis - are noteworthy and worth revisiting. For example, in his essay, ‘Language and Time in Citizen Kane’, written in 1998, speaking about the moment Susan leaves Kane, Corel writes: “The camera pans to the right to frame a black empty space, which reveals itself to be a mirror when Kane appears and is reflected in an endless number of images. We have before our eyes the birth of the narcissistic grandiose mirror image arising in place of a severe loss.” (Psychoanal. Inq.,18,2:154:160, Corel MD.) In the International Journal, he published ‘Theo Angelopoulos’ film Ulysses’ Gaze,’ (Corel, 2004, Int. J. Psychoanal. 85, 4:1017-1021, and ‘Repetition and Surprise: Construction and its Validation’ (1990, Int. J. Psychoanal.71:411-420, Faimberg and Corel).
