responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Exports in the School Setting
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Exports in the School Setting

Alicia Kachinovsky

Alicia Kachinovsky

alicia.kachinovsky@gmail.com
Montevideo, Uruguay
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-1611-6329

Alicia Kachinovsky  

Exported psychoanalysis; educational institution; children’s literature; abstinence; second look

This article describes and substantiates some of the requirements for how a psychoanalyst should respond to the demands of an educational institution in order to constitute best practices. It considers the following requirements: the need to renounce the position of assigned expert (paradox of an expert/ignorant), the position of outsider that must be held, the specificity of the principle of abstention, and the benefits of appealing to a second look. This second look includes an analysis of the analyst's involvement, while the analyst is inserted in a society whose discourses could parasitise their thinking. These four requirements are illustrated in an experience carried out in public schools in a context of social vulnerability. The clinical device created and applied in these settings is a narrative workshop that uses children's literature as linking objects. The example described here is limited to one instance of the use of the device and, in particular, to the process and the difficulties of working with a six-year-old girl who participated in one of the workshops. The changes she experienced are revealed through an analysis of an observed phoric function and its vicissitudes. This case allows us to note the complexity of the transference dynamics that must be sustained.

responseSet.title: Editorial for Issue 2, 2022
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial for Issue 2, 2022

Francis Grier

Francis Grier

fjrgrier@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3757-852X

Francis Grier  

I am greatly honoured to become Editor-In-Chief of the International Journal. I have been involved with the IJP since 2005, reviewing many papers on a whole host of different subjects and from all the different regions of psychoanalysis, and from 2017 becoming the UK Regional Editor. Since then I have worked very closely with the other editors, especially with Dana Birksted-Breen.

I owe Dana an enormous debt of gratitude. Most of what I have learned about editing I have learned from her, and over the years I have also been absorbing her love of international psychoanalytic scholarship, her curiosity about the myriad, different forms that psychoanalytic theory and clinical work take in different regions of the world and in different theoretical groupings, the immense influence of language and culture on different psychoanalytic dialects and ways of communicating, ways of collaborating and indeed ways of fighting. Dana has a remarkable ability to combine a kind of respectful empathy and tolerance, with an exceedingly sharp capacity for critique. She has insisted on the crucial importance of weighing up the quality of argumentation in any paper, so that, for example, it is possible to admire the positive level of argumentation even when one may hold strongly conflicting views from the author’s, and, contrariwise, although one may feel oneself to be close to an author’s attitude, one may have to perceive that the author’s argumentation is nevertheless of poor quality.

This is at the heart of the mission of the International Journal, to set out within one journal very different expressions of psychoanalytic issues and, even, of psychoanalysis itself. Our field has become fragmented, dangerously so, so that it is now very difficult for different psychoanalytic schools to engage meaningfully with one another. Much engagement happens within psychoanalytic groupings, which to some extent are co-terminous with geographical areas, and leads to a sense of people only talking to each other if they share the same view. The international aspect of the IJP is precisely about the attempt to get conversations - and arguments - going between different geographical and theoretical/clinical areas of psychoanalysis, side by side. This should be the lifeblood of psychoanalysis, procuring its growth and development. This has always been the prime vocation of the IJP, since its founding by Freud and Jones in 1919, but never has the need been more urgent.

responseSet.title: Freud's Why War? Revisited
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Why War? Revisited

Viviane Chetrit-Vatine

Viviane Chetrit-Vatine

chetritvatineviviane@gmail.com

Viviane Chetrit-Vatine  

Ethics; war; death; peace; responsibility; sacred; third; life

Alternating a return to Freud’s propositions in the well-known paper “Why War?” revisited with her own hypothesis of a feminine-maternal matricial third sitting at the origin of ethics conceived as responsibility towards the other, the author refers to Levinas’s reflections on the phenomenon of war. She relates in particular to his introduction of Totality and Infinity, to passages from Difficult Freedom, as well as to one of his later works, which, in an interesting resonance with contemporary violence, is called: From the Sacred to the Holy. While contributing clinical illustrations or elements connected with the Israeli socio-political context in which she practices, the author focuses on the tension between the reality of wars and the aspiration for peace which she sees as related to a nostalgia to intimacy, that is to the feminine-maternal residing in every human.

responseSet.title: The Case of J: Returning to the Office
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Case of J: Returning to the Office

Bernard Chervet

Bernard Chervet

bernard@chervet.fr
16 rue Jacques Callot, 75006 Paris, France

Bernard Chervet  

Before commenting on the clinical material that has given rise to a written text, it is necessary to recall two general points. The analyst who writes a text operates in very different psychic conditions from those that obtain when they are listening to their patients during a session. Writing a text thus requires supplementary work, for which I would like to thank the author. Moreover, the commentator only has access to the text and not to the patient. The account expresses the “patient as an internal object” constructed by the patient's analyst, a construction that thus reflects the reality of the patient and that of the analyst's countertransference. It is therefore important for the commentator not to confuse the text and the patient.

Context, Absence and Presence

J.'s sessions took place in person in the analyst's usual office after a long period, 14 months, of online sessions. This physical distancing was necessary due to the risks of being contaminated by COVID-19 and because of the lockdown measures put in place.

This situation might be considered as an experimental opportunity set up artificially under the constraint of elements external to the analysis itself. The sensorial relationship between the analyst and the analysand was therefore much more reduced and concentrated on a single sense - acoustic. It is probable that this sensorial channel drained all the other senses and represented them because they were impeded. During the lockdown and the setting-up of remote sessions, this restriction and focalization were accompanied by experiences of intense solitude, lack, frustration, sadness and hope for a better future, all of which were expressed by J. The analyst, for his part, tells us that he/she often felt relieved at the end of the sessions to find himself/herself alone in an empty office next to an empty couch. The analysand was deprived of the sensorial presence of his analyst, and thus of close attention. The reminiscences that can be inferred from this situation are those of a mother who is more or less dreamy (rêverie), distracted or attentive, situated near her child (or not), a child who is themself busy with some activity of day-dreaming or play.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Autistic States in Children by Frances Tustin, London and New York, Routledge, 3rd ed., 1992/2021, $19.50 (paperback); $131.75 (hardback), 283 pp., ISBN: 978-0-367-54735-6

Mary Brady

Mary Brady

San Francisco, CA, USA
mbrady66@earthlink.net
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-9128-1810

Mary Brady  

What is the relevance of Tustin’s classic work Autistic States in Children (newly reissued in the Routledge Classics series) to child and adult psychoanalysts today?

I would offer that her concept of “encapsulation” (in extreme and lesser forms, along with her clinical approach to such protective yet harmful defences) as well as her observations of early bodily terrors (and accompanying somatic defences) would be valuable for any clinician. In her Foreword to the new edition Maria Rhode notes that Tustin saw her primary contribution as

understanding the sensation-based mechanisms that protect the child with autism from what has felt like a catastrophic realization of bodily separateness … However, turning for protection to self-generated sensations prevents the child from being open to help from other human beings. (xvii)

Rhode notes that, more broadly speaking, Tustin saw “autistic self-protective strategies as part of the human condition - tragically restrictive, though felt [by us!] to be necessary” (xvii).

I undertook this review with the understanding that I would approach this classic as a child and adult analyst with a general practice, not as an expert on the treatment of autistic children. I knew I could benefit from thinking more deeply about what Tustin calls “pockets of autism” in patients who would not receive an autism spectrum disorder (ASD) diagnosis, but for and with whom I have grappled with autistic states and defences.

Tustin combines full-blown forms of autistic encapsulation and confusional entanglement together under the term “childhood psychosis”. Diagnostically, I did not find this helpful as ASDs are today generally considered neurodevelopmental. Tustin, however, is speaking from a Kleinian rather than a psychiatric perspective. From a Kleinian view, both autistic encapsulation and confusional entanglement yield an inability to face reality - fundamental to the Kleinian conception of psychotic process. From a heuristic point of view, I found the juxtaposition of encapsulation and confusional entanglement useful in colouring in the divergent ways children develop.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Not a literal translation… In fact, rather performative - A review of Translation/Transformation: 100 Years of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis edited by D. Birksted-Breen, London, Routledge, 2021, pp. 337.

Guiseppe Civitarese

Guiseppe Civitarese

gcivitarese@gmail.com

Guiseppe Civitarese  

I have been a subscriber to IJP, if my memory serves me well, for at least thirty years. A row of its volumes was magnificently displayed on my analyst’s bookshelf. To me they were the expression of prestige and belonging to an elite group. My memory, entirely personal and subjective, was of an aristocratic journal, authoritative but also very “British” and very “Establishment”. It is incredible how it has changed over time. Today, if we pick up any issue of the IJP we immediately realize that it has become a true home for analysts from all parts of the world and of any theoretical orientation, not only because of the great number of people involved in the various editing phases and the invaluable translations of the best articles featured in the various Annuals, but also for the scope and wide variety of its entries. This change has occurred without compromising the journal’s prestige, which has, if anything, increased, thanks to recent directors, in particular Dana Birksted-Breen, who has recently finished her term.

As the subtitle indicates, Translation/Transformation, edited by Dana Birksted-Breen herself, celebrates 100 years of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis. Naturally, the anniversary is of significance to the world of psychoanalysis. We know that celebratory rites are important for group identity, but this could have been a lacklustre volume, blandly celebrative, of the kind that attracts an indifferent eye, and immediately dismissed. This is not the case for this book. The twelve essays, several of which have already appeared in the journal and are collected into two sections, are interesting, easy to read and thought-provoking, and also offer the occasion to assess the current state of art.

The book is divided into two parts. The first section contains writings of an eminently historic character: on Freud’s passion for archaeology (Heinz and Carina Weiss), the history of the foundation of the IJP (Georg Bruns), the English translation of Freud’s works (Riccardo Steiner, Dee McQuillan), and people who have played an important role in the life of the journal such as Ernest Jones, Joan Riviere (Rachel Blass) and Marjorie Brierley (Nellie Thompson).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Academia: The Case of Angel Garma and the University of Buenos Aires

Miguel Huertas-Maestro , Silvia Lévy and Rafael Huertas

Rafael Huertas

miguel.huertas@csic.es
Madrid, Spain
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-8513-2293

Barcelona, Spain
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-2853-2565

Madrid, Spain
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-4543-7180

Rafael Huertas  

Ángel Garma, University of Buenos Aires, history of psychoanalysis

The Spanish psychiatrist and psychoanalyst Ángel Garma—exiled in Argentina after the Spanish Civil War—was one of the founders and the first president of the Argentinian Psychoanalytical Association. Garma unsuccessfully tried to become a university lecturer on three occasions. His final attempt was in 1965, when he applied for a professorship in deep psychology at the Faculty of Philosophy and Literature of the University of Buenos Aires. The application he submitted for this professorship position has been located in Ángel Garma’s personal archive. The aim of this article is to analyse this manuscript to search for clues about the author’s relationship with his own work and to explain the priorities and interests he intended to explore in the university environment. The first part of the article analyses the content of his application for the professorship, contrasting this content with published work and reviewing the fundamental lines of Garma’s psychoanalytical thought (child sexuality, psychoanalysis and psychosomatic medicine, the psychoanalytical institution, etc.). The second part suggests the main reasons why Garma failed in his attempts to become a university lecturer.

responseSet.title: The Case of J: Returning to the Office
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Case of J: Returning to the Office

Anonymous

Anonymous

Catherine.Humble@iopa.org.uk

Anonymous  

June 01, 2021 (Tuesday)

First session after returning to the office-

J missed the first two weeks of sessions after I reopened my office. He was out visiting his family during this time. He arrived for his session on time on Tuesday since Monday was a public holiday. I greeted him: “Good afternoon J.” He replied looking at me: “Good afternoon Dr. Y.”

We walked to the office, with me walking behind him. He laid down on the couch, after leaving his keys and cellphone on the other chair as if a year and a half had not elapsed since he was last in this room. I sat down on my chair behind the couch.)

A short pause

P: Wild and crazy to be back in person

I am nervous now

Now that I am here it feels super familiar. Almost as if the last 15 months did not happen. I know this room. It is so familiar. It is kind of strange how familiar it feels.

When I came in, I had a little trouble at the gate. I had forgotten how to open it.

Then my mind started to mark. That’s here, that’s not.

I am struck by how much the smell in the house reminds me of my grandparent’s apt. Dad’s dad and stepmother. It felt like I was walking into that apt. Adds a strangeness…the two things are rubbed together in a funny way.

[[RunningHead]]

The Contemporary Freudian tradition: Past and present, Edited by Ken Robinson and Joan Schachter, Routledge, Abingdon, Oxon and New York, NY, 2021, $44.95 (paperback edition), ISBN: 978-0-367-48356-2 (pbk)

Sharone Bergner

Sharone Bergner

sbergnerphd@gmail.com
139 West 82nd Street, Suite 1CD, New York, NY 10024, USA

Sharone Bergner  

This generous volume offers three gifts: diverse, interesting papers representing the evolution of Freud’s work as understood by one of the major groups in British psychoanalysis, a wonderful introduction that is itself a master class, and an invitation to consider both what unifies Contemporary Freudians while also setting them apart within British psychoanalysis, as well as 922 what this might help us understand about contemporary Freudians elsewhere in our broader psychoanalytic community. The book includes a mix of theoretically elucidating papers and fascinating clinical material, which combine to flesh out a way of thinking and working rooted in core longstanding Freudian ideas and fertilized by contributions from other groups within the British Society. The collection shows that the thinking of British Contemporary Freudians, having evolved in relation to both Freudian tradition and ideas shaping its recent and current neighbourhood, is characterized by both depth and an either/or-defying breadth. What comes through is substance and aliveness.

In the masterful introduction, editors Ken Robinson and Joan Schacter tell the story of evolution and diversity within the British Contemporary Freudian group, with its roots in Freud and in Anna Freud’s extensions and developmentally anchored work at the Hampstead Clinic, which was a centre of Freudian thought after the Freuds’ emigration from Vienna. Some British Contemporary Freudian analysts are based in Anna Freud’s extensions of Freud, some are more rooted in cross-fertilization with the work of analysts from other groups within the British society, such as Winnicott and Klein, while others utilize a kind of direct return to Freud synthetic with the work of French psychoanalysts, or are focused on ideas shared with Italian psychoanalysts writing about the body.

To capture this diversity, the editors offer the Wittgensteinian concept of “family resemblance”. The idea of “family resemblance” is flexible and elastic, allowing the editors to distinguish central concerns that define particular “families” of psychoanalysts. Members of a particular “family” share a sufficient number of, but not all, core concerns or resemblances. Some - an interest in the unconscious, for example - are more prominent, both theoretically and in clinical usage, among members of the Contemporary Freudian family. The editors use this idea as a frame within which to house and to highlight the group’s commonalities as well as divergences, both within it and in its relation to the other groups in British psychoanalysis, which were the heirs to the divisive “Controversial Discussions” of 1942-44.

[[RunningHead]]

Some Thoughts about Transgenderism and Gender Dysphoria

Per Anthi

Per Anthi

Oslo, Norway
anthi@online.no
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-6153-0636

Per Anthi  

I find the introduction by Raphael Blass and the discussion between David Bell and Avgi Saketopoulou very interesting (see International Journal of Psychoanalysis, vol. 101, 1014-1039, 2020). First, Bell opens his presentation with a maxim from Wilhelm Reich’s writing: Every social order creates those character forms which it needs for its own preservation … The character structure … is the crystallization of the sociological process of a given epoch. Bell does not say explicitly what he means by this citation, nor does he clarify the context in which Reich applies this maxim.

One will find these sentences in the first preface to his book Character Analysis from 1933 (Anthi, 2020). But I think the basic premises for this maxim were laid in his book Die Massenpsychologie des Faschismus, also from 1933. This represented the first sociological study founded on psychoanalytic theory. I would appreciate it if Bell would explain his reasons for giving prominence to Reich’s original observations of how a ruling political regime in a given society could be preserved by cultivating specific social structures and character types, and thus, for example, maintain an authoritarian regime. In my opinion, Bell’s sociological considerations should be supplied by Bauman (2000) and his sociological theories about the modern (postmodern) society and its liquid forms where children and adolescents, to a greater extent than before, are left to themselves in a digitalized internet universe lacking normative frames and symbolically meaningful common structures. The circumstances while growing up are marked by unclear borders while the need for confirmation of one’s identity becomes importunate.

Although persons born as intersex are very rare, they can nevertheless demonstrate the significance of how psychoanalytic treatment can contribute to give them a more functional and usable identity related to how they feel regarding their gender. For many years ago I had in analysis an intersex patient. The analysis strengthened his identity as a man and helped him to reconcile himself with his defect genitalia. The treatment represented an effective remedy against his depressive illness, and he became able to utilize his intellectual and creative resources, choosing a vocation that reinforced his phallic identifications and feeling of masculinity.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Translating Case Material and Session Protocols

Matthias von der Tann

Matthias von der Tann

mvondertann@gmail.com

Matthias von der Tann  

Letter to the Editor of the IJP

Dear Editor,

It is with great interest that I read the papers composing the “Analyst at Work” section in issue 2/2021 of the IJP. I would like to add an issue for consideration which I felt has not having been given sufficient attention.

The problems in the translation of psychoanalytic papers and books - whether by Freud or others - into another language (in the beginning from German into English) have been widely studied. This does not seem to be the case for issues around translations of case material in clinical presentations into another language. And yet these translations are an essential step in the preparation of the communication of clinical material in a language different from the original one.

These texts, usually having been translated into English, have become a normal part of many presentations for different purposes. The material has therefore been through one more than the usual number of transformations before being presented. For the sake of simplicity I only mention two steps after the transformation of unconscious material into the communication in the session. The analyst first transforms the original material (what “actually happened” in the session) into a written text (step 1). For the presentation this text is then being translated into a different language - either by the analyst themself or by a translator (step 2). Step 1 has been widely studied, and there is a wide spectrum about the best way to communicate (e.g. to a supervisor) what happened in a session. Of course these presentations always contain much more than the famous “exchange of words”. Step 2 seems to have gained less attention although every presentation and supervision conducted in a language different from the original encounter has to undergo this transformation. The number of translations used in this regard probably ranges in the tens of thousands.

I would like to invite the reader to have a look at how these translations usually seem to be conducted, and I would like to offer some questions in this regard arising from the case material of Yune. We do not know in the beginning whether Wangku Rho is a male or a female analyst. We also do not have any information about the language in which this analysis has been conducted. Assuming that Korean is Yune’s as well as Wangku’s mother tongue and that the analysis was conducted in Korean, the published text would then be a translation from Korean into English. How might that have been carried out and - more importantly - what might the translation have inherited and what might have got lost from the original text without that being directly apparent?

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Paradigms for “Internal” Change in the Institutions
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Paradigms for “Internal” Change in the Institutions**

Christophe Bittolo and Cécile Corre

Cécile Corre

http://orcid.org/0000-0002-7242-6524
christophebittolo@me.com
19 Bd Vauban, 78180 Montigny le Bretonneux, France

France

Cécile Corre  

Applied psychoanalysis, groups, empirical research

This article reflects on the transmission of clinical practices based on psychoanalytic theories and methods in care institutions. It examines the issues raised by these practices and the impact on therapists in the French social, scientific, and economic context. We attempt to identify the factors that promote the implementation of a process of reflection on established practices and their evolution.

Our hypothesis is that any process of transmission of psychoanalytic practices in institutions implies thinking about the institution as an object of psychic reality, and grappling with potentially persecuting elements of change; these include the upheaval of values and ideals underlying the practices, as well as generational transmissions and their potential for conflict. The possibility of working on these internal objects raises the question of the institutional capacity for containing conflict; it also involves thinking about the nature and function of institutional spaces.

Three points in particular are developed: the organizational modes of structures and their impact; the history of the structure and its intergenerational transmission; and the place of third parties in the process of historicization and transmission. These three points define the objects that are collectively elaborated, and the method that promotes the evolution and creativity of psychoanalytic practices in institutions.

responseSet.title: Migration and Integration in the Internal and External Community: Narcissistic Defence Organizations as a Hindrance to Integration
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Migration and Integration in the Internal and External Community: Narcissistic Defence Organizations as a Hindrance to Integration

Kristin White

Kristin White

Berlin, Germany
Kristin.White@t-online.de

Berlin, Germany

Gotha, Germany

Kristin White  

Community, narcissistic defence organisation, migration, integration, Herbert Rosenfeld, Alfred Adler

In this paper, I am concerned with the question of relationship between internal personality integration and external integration in the community within the context of voluntary migration. Migration always includes a loss of all that one has left behind. Both internal and external integration in the new community thus involve a mourning process that involves a working through of depressive position anxieties. Only then can the migrant turn towards the new object, the new community and initiate a process of integration. This process is particularly difficult for patients with an entrenched system of narcissistic defences, in which they have turned away from the helpful object, also the helpful object of the community around them and the helpful object of the analyst. These are often patients who have used the migration as a kind of psychic retreat. The difficult process of integration is illustrated in this paper with the treatment of Mr B.

responseSet.title: Summary Page, Issue 2, 2022
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Summary Page, Issue 2, 2022

Editorial

Francis Grier, the new Editor-in-Chief of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis, thanks the editors and consultants of the IJP, and looks forward to a flourishing future for the journal.

Psychoanalytic Theory and Technique

Drawing on Freud's conception of “attention” and Bion's emphasis on the power of observation, Avner Bergstein reflects on the analyst's attempt to approach the unrepressed unconscious and explores the clinical implications of “intuition.”

Tracing the origin of Bion’s concept of “extension” in the work of the philosopher Alfred North Whitehead, Didier Houzel argues that “extension” is central to Bion’s exploration of psychic growth and the analytic process, in particular the transference. The concept of “extension” helps to dissect the role of the analyst’s intuition, argues Houzel.

In her examination of remote analysis, Stefanie Sedlacek argues that it can promote regression to idealised object images, but previously repressed biographical memories can also be experienced in the “double virtuality” and so worked through. With the distance and presence fostered by remote analysis, the psychic reality of unfulfilled longing can be made palpable.

Berdj Papazian examines the transgenerational roots of somatising mechanisms in “bubble babies,” or children who are “neutropenic” or develop arthritis. These transgenerational pathologies are triggered by obstacles in oedipal structuring, a denied symbolic castration, and destructive aggression occurs, with possible epigenetic components. Papazian postulates a splitting of the primordial self at the level of the proto-mental.

History of Psychoanalysis

Between 1920 and 1925 a shift occurred in psychoanalytic theory that related primarily to the status of aggression. Ulrike May explores the European authors who participated in this change, and to what extent they prepared the ground for the reception of Melanie Klein’s work on aggression. The author also looks at how Freud distanced himself from this departure from the primacy of the sexual.

responseSet.title: Introduction: The Case of J - Returning to the Office
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction: The Case of J - Returning to the Office

Abbot Bronstein

Abbot Bronstein

San Francisco

Abbot Bronstein  

In the Analyst at Work section, we hope to see how an analyst works within the privacy of the consulting room. In this present section, we diverge from our usual practice and have asked an analyst, Dr Y, to present a second time in rather short succession. She has very generously opened her clinical work for our examination to the IJP community and to the four discussants. The first AAW section explored the move to remote analysis in the early phase of the Covid 19 pandemic. We now hear the same patient almost two years later. Both analyst and patient have weathered the pandemic, meeting via phone.

In these three sessions we see the analyst and patient return to in-person meetings. Although the issue of phone/remote psychoanalysis is a central issue in training and clinical practice at this juncture in analytic history, we are also in a somewhat different, “forced” situation with the pandemic. Many papers are being written on the subject of both the pandemic and the questions about distant analysis.

Dr Y’s case and the four discussions affords us a chance to study both of these complicated and important issues in our field today. As the editor of this section, I am very grateful to the analyst, to the discussants, and to the editorial readers who have worked on this AAW section on such short notice and with such enthusiasm.

CONTACT Abbot Bronstein abbot.bronstein@gmail.com

responseSet.title: Red O'Shaughnessy, Our Friendship
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Red O'Shaughnessy, Our Friendship

Jonathan Lear

Jonathan Lear

Chicago, USA
jlear@uchicago.edu
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-1982-8759

Jonathan Lear  

We are in a car driving through the neighborhoods of Chicago. Sometimes we are taking in the sights - the tracks of the El we are driving under, the Lake that stretches out 300 miles - but other times we are filling each other in on our life stories. At some point Red said, “This is a mighty city”. I could feel the poetic force of her utterance: this was a city from which one could gather strength. Of course, with a name like O'Shaughnessy, red hair and a nickname to go with it, one might expect that “mighty” would have been pronounced with an Irish accent - much as one does hear at O'Shaughnessy's Public House in north Chicago. But no.

The car, the drive, the conversation created its own intimate space. Neither of us had an interest in analyzing our narratives further, but we were curious about the how of our friendship. We had a sense of each other's present-day qualities, but was there an historical dimension of which we were not aware? What was the from which of our lives? There were common themes. Both of our families had forced emigrations in previous generations, Jewish families escaping pogroms in eastern Europe (hers to South Africa, mine to Connecticut). There were absent or failed fathers and families falling apart. And then there was a second, personal emigration each with a double-magic: hers from South Africa to Oxford, mine from New England to Cambridge University. We each left a scene of misery and transported ourselves over geographical space. (The inner relief of such movement still needs to be better understood.) We each ended up at great British Universities where, in some sense, our adult lives began. We each were drawn to philosophy and then to psychoanalysis - cultures of inquiry with which one could fall in love and identify. We struck different balances but there was a common call to which we responded.

531

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Francis Grier

Francis Grier

fjrgrier@gmail.com
0000-0003-3757-852X

Francis Grier  

I would like to comment on changes we have just introduced in the way that professional group subscribers to the International Journal can gain access to issues and articles.

The traditional method of reading the print copy of the Journal naturally remains. However, we know that a growing number of subscribers are comfortable accessing the Journal digitally via the web, and also that there are growing concerns about the environmental costs of printing and shipping. Additionally, during the pandemic there were severe disruptions to postal services, so even if subscribers want printed copies, it can also be useful to be able to access the Journal digitally (and also to search it and find content). Because the digital version has become so important (in 2022 60% of our subscribers have already chosen a digital-only subscription), we have taken steps to make it much more accessible.

Subscribers in universities and public institutions will continue to access the Journal via T&F online - the digital platform of our publisher, Taylor & Francis, with which they are familiar. For them there is no change. However, we know that the great majority of psychoanalysts and other professionals interested in psychoanalysis, worldwide, have not been using their digital subscription at T&F online, perhaps because they do not use publisher websites ordinarily, and also because it requires them to register and remember specialist access codes and web addresses. What this group ordinarily access for psychoanalytic material is PEP-Web - the Psychoanalytic Electronic Publishing archive of over 70 journals, as well as books and videos, to which over 22,000 professionals worldwide subscribe.

Until now, PEP has been including the full texts of papers from all the leading journals as they are published, but contracts between PEP and publishers forbade full-text access for PEP archive subscribers for three years or a bit more. Because we knew that very few people actually took the trouble to read the Journal through the Taylor & Francis site, it has meant that a large proportion of the psychoanalytic readership was always, in effect, three years behind, with regard to articles published in current issues. We even found that many authors would not search for papers linked to their topics in current issues of the Journal. This situation obviously militated against the desirable position of authors and readers being as up to date and on the cutting-edge of publications and psychoanalytic thought as possible, pushing instead in a conservative direction with authors and readers lagging three years behind what was being published in the immediate present.

[[RunningHead]]

Elizabeth Severn: the “evil genius” of psychoanalysis, by Arnold Rachman, New York, Routledge, 2018, 234 pp, $48.95, ISBN: 978-1-138-12286-4

Romy Reading

Romy Reading

romy@romyreading.com
80 Fifth Avenue, Suite 1406, New York, NY 10011

Romy Reading  

In this book, a volume within the Psychoanalytic Inquiry Book Series edited by Joseph D. Lichtenberg, Arnold Rachman, psychoanalyst and psychohistorian, compiles a wealth of archival research on Elizabeth Severn and brings forth a rediscovery of one of the most controversial figures of early psychoanalysis. In a concerted effort to rehabilitate both Severn's reputation and the historical account of Sandor Ferenczi's work with her, Dr. Rachman offers a revisionist narrative of their clinical encounter and of Severn's professional life. He also attempts to trace the origins of many key features of contemporary psychoanalysis to Ferenczi's pioneering analysis with Severn, whom he understands to be both a severely traumatized and troubled patient and an insightful and highly intelligent clinical peer of Ferenczi's. Additionally, Dr. Rachman presents the story of his own meticulous and painstaking efforts to retrieve and preserve the Elizabeth Severn Papers, thereby illuminating the complexities of archival review, historical preservation, and dissidence within the field of psychoanalysis.

Dr. Rachman begins by presenting the concept of Todschweigen, which he translates as “death by silence”. This German term, which was suggested to him by his mentor Esther Menaker, describes the long-standing practice of the institutional silencing of theoretical dissidents and individuals who speak out about abuses within the profession. Dr. Rachman situates the presentation of this construct within the narration of his personal experience of navigating authoritarian supervisors and institutional politics while a psychoanalytic candidate in the 1960s. Infused with a desire to challenge hegemonic practices within the profession, Dr. Rachman takes risks by disclosing difficult personal experiences with analysts and supervisors that, for him, exemplify Todschweigen and the oft-described history of authoritarianism in the 914

responseSet.title: Letter to the Editor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter to the Editor

David Bell

David Bell

davidlbell@hotmail.com

David Bell  

I would like to thank Per Anthi very much for his most interesting contribution. I think he is quite right that I do not explain properly the reason for the Reich quotation - and I am grateful for the details about its provenance. I agree its relevance is left somewhat allusive, and I welcome this opportunity to make it a bit clearer.

In the paper I mention the penetration of the commodity form into ever-more aspects of our lives; this is an aspect of the ever-increasing hegemony of neoliberalism. An important manifestation of this cultural shift is found in the way that even aspects of personhood can acquire, in the mind of the subject, a commoditized form. In my view, this transformation, which affects the way we think about ourselves and the world we move in, has not come out of the blue but serves certain dominant interests. The point, then, that I am making is that this changed culture is part of the soil in which transgender ideology has germinated so rapidly.

I agree completely about the relevance of Bauman's works to this whole discussion. I was also interested to hear about this treatment of an intersex case which must be extremely rare, and I urge Per Anthi to write this up. Lastly, I was fascinated to hear about Hemingway, most of which was new to me.

David Bell

The British Institute of Psychoanalysis

davidlbell@hotmail.com

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Chasing Infinity: Why Clinical Psychoanalysis' Future Lies in Pluralism

Alan Karbelnig

Alan Karbelnig

amkarbelnig@gmail.com
117 E. Colorado Boulevard, Suite 425, Pasadena, CA 91105, USA
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-5376-052X

2014 Sawtelle Boulevard, Los Angeles, CA 90025, USA

Alan Karbelnig  

Psychoanalysis; pluralism; psychoanalytic psychotherapy; psychodynamics; Wallerstein; Greenberg

Part of an ongoing political and scholarly effort to move the psychoanalytic project towards greater cohesiveness, the author proposes a standard method, borrowing the phrase from physics, for a structured, eclectic clinical pluralism. She [or he] uses psychoanalytic and philosophical arguments, as well as considerations of patients’ personal idiosyncrasies, to conclude that achieving one, over-arching metapsychology will prove impossible. In other words, and despite their ardent efforts, psychoanalytic theorists, including Freud (1895/1991), Rangell (1975, 2006), Greenberg and Mitchell (1983), Wallerstein (1988, 1990, 2002, 2005, 2013), and others, failed to create models accounting for the dynamically interacting variables creating human subjectivity. Their struggles to bridge psychoanalytic theories of mind and method also fell short. As a result, the author suggests, clinical psychoanalysis’ ultimate fate lies in organizing what Wallerstein (2005) called the “common ground” (p. 626) into methodology which invites psychoanalytic practitioners to mine the psychoanalytic opus for its “plethora of theoretical metaphors” (Wallerstein, 2013, p. 36), “controlling fictions” (Greenberg, 2015, p. 17), “useful untruths” (Lament, 2020, p. 196), or “regional dialects” (Fulgencio, 2020, p. 15). Cohesively applying a pluralistically based metapsychology to guide clinical processes, rather than provide maps of the mind, offers much-needed unity to psychoanalysis’ applied wing.

Björn Salomonsson  

Parent-infant psychotherapy, psychoanalysis, metaphor, technique

This paper investigates a phenomenon observed in parent-infant psychotherapy (PIP). Metaphors emerge in the analyst and, once voiced, they can become tools for understanding the present predicament of mother and/or child. The article contains vignettes from work with a mother and her son, four weeks old when PIP started. They are followed by a vignette of an adult analysand. In both settings, the analyst found himself in an impasse, until he came up with a metaphor expressed to the mother and the analysand, respectively. The paper investigates why PIP experiences might inspire an analyst to suggest metaphors to adult patients as well and thence to understand their suffering better. Aspects of linguistic theory underlining the infantile roots of metaphors are submitted as well as other analysts’ views of using metaphors at work. It describes how the validity of a metaphor - whether it expresses something essential about the patient’s internal world - should be assessed by following up his/her response to it. It defends the position that metaphor, if used with parsimony and sobriety, is a valuable tool in enabling the patient to map their internal world.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Constitutive-Intervention”- Structuring Primal Psycho-Physical Space

Tami Pollak

Tami Pollak

Tel Aviv, Israel
Shayont54@gmail.com
8 Bikurey Tzion st’, Jaffa 6818564

Tami Pollak  

Constitutive intervention, autism, psychophysical structure, pre-symbolic communication

This paper describes a specific type of therapeutic intervention relevant to therapeutic communication with patients exhibiting non-symbolic autistic defenses or traumatic characteristics. The paper focuses on clinical manifestations of developmental deficiencies and communication problems that stem from a primal psychophysical space that is unmatured, traumatized, deformed and/or chaotic. The concept of the body-container is presented as a proto-scheme of primal psychophysical space, organizing the intersections of one's anatomical structure and potentials for intersubjectivity.

This conceptual context allows the therapist to conceive the patient's non-representational and non-communicative physical actions in the therapeutic setting as presence-in-action. Such presence is discussed as a crucial invitation for the emergence of the therapist's intuitive pre-symbolic creativity, which may eventually crystallize into a perceptually-presented 'constitutive intervention'. This unique kind of intervention is designed to support the patient's creative capacity for pre-symbolic realization of this multi-dimensional psychophysical proto-scheme. Various constitutive interventions, utilizing concrete gestures that involve the therapist's body and the physical aspect of the therapeutic environment, are examined theoretically and illustrated through clinical vignettes.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Approaches to a Contemporary Psychoanalytic Field Theory: From Kurt Lewin, Georges Politzer and José Bleger, to Antonino Ferro and Giuseppe Civitarese**

Danielle Bazzi

Danielle Bazzi

dbazzi@dplanet.ch
Quellenstrasse 27 CH-8005, Zürich, Switzerland

Danielle Bazzi  

Psychoanalytical field theory, epistemology, history of psychoanalysis

Today in Italy, a group of psychoanalysts works according to Field Theory. In this paper I would like to show that a line of thought from the beginnings of Field Theory in physics, philosophy and psychology leads to the conception of the field of Antonino Ferro and Giuseppe Civitarese. The assumption of a continuous dynamic and a direct relationship between all parts of the field is fundamental for the conception of the analytic field. These parts have a field-dependent identity and are different in sum than when the parts are taken on their own. To trace the evolution of field-theoretical psychoanalysis, I describe the epistemological context in which Kurt Lewin developed Field Theory. I will add selected theoretical elements from works by Georges Politzer and José Bleger.

responseSet.title: Reflections on masochism: An Introduction
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reflections on masochism: An Introduction

Catalina Bronstein

Catalina Bronstein

London

Catalina Bronstein  

Masochism, sadism, life and death drives

Masochism is central to all pathologies and its relevance in clinical practise cannot be underestimated. The initial connection made by Freud was that masochism was a component or partial instinct, still operating within the pleasure principle. The relationship between masochism and the theory of drives marks a main theoretical difference in the different authors' explorations of this subject. The understanding of what is meant by ‘masochism' gained complexity following Freud's postulation of a life and death drive (which is more or less contemporary with his 1924 paper on masochism) and the differences made by him between ‘primary' and ‘secondary’ masochism. This introduction to the papers presented in this section will address some of these differences, as well as exploring the notions of primary erotogenic masochism, feminine and moral masochism. It will also look at the notion of binding /unbinding of the life/death drives, and the role of the superego. It will introduce the different papers by Novick and Novick, Bourdin, Frank and Persano on developmental perspectives, primary masochism, views on French analysts such as Benno Rosenberg and on Kleinian ideas on the subject as well as on the role of the body, pain and self harm.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytical Intuition and Reverie: Capturing Facts Not Yet Dreamed

Marina Ferreira da Rosa Ribeiro

Marina Ferreira da Rosa Ribeiro

http://orcid.org/0000-0002-2278-063X
marinaribeiro@usp.br
Prof. Mello de Moraes 1721 Bloco F, São Paulo, 05508030 Brazil

Marina Ferreira da Rosa Ribeiro  

This article promotes a dialogue some of Bion’s concepts and those of post-Bionian psychoanalysts (Ogden, Ferro, Rocha Barros and Chuster), looking in particular at psychoanalytic intuition, reverie and the alpha function. How can we think about the connection between reverie and intuition? Could the analyst’s state of reverie have at its centre - before and beyond the sensuous, in the infra- and ultra-sensuous - the analyst’s capacity for intuition? The paper presents a disturbing experience of an analyst in her consulting room, looking at how the concepts work in the clinical material. The clinical material sustains the hypothesis that reverie is an evolution of psychoanalytic intuition, and that intuition occurs between caesuras, which is supported by Bion's proposal of no memory, no desire, no prior understanding, that is, negative capacity.

responseSet.title: Masochism
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Masochism**

Dominique Bourdin

Dominique Bourdin

dominique@bourdin.biz

Dominique Bourdin  

Drive, masochism, primary masochism, moral masochism, passivity, perversion, regression, sadism

This text presents how Freud thinks about the masochism. In 1915, it is the return of active sadism in a passive drive which is characteristic of masochism, always secondary. In 1919, he sees in masochism the genesis of perversions. And the article of 1924 knows a primary masochism and distinguishes erotogenic masochism, feminine masochism and moral masochism. After Freud, many studies of clinical forms of masochism can be noted, and in France there is a thesis about the masochism as a first psychic link (Benno Rosenberg, Marilia Aisenstein).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Environmental Crisis and the Film First Reformed: Paths to Paralysis and Change

Christopher W. T. Miller , Lindsay L. Clarkson and Donald R. Ross

Donald R. Ross

cwtmiller@gmail.com
2120 L St NW Ste 600-1, Washington, DC 20037
701 W. Pratt St, 4th floor, Baltimore, MD 21201, USA
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-6236-0357

Washington

Baltimore

Baltimore, MD, USA

Donald R. Ross  

Natural world, object relations, environmental crisis

The natural world has always been integral to our existence; currently, we are compelled to reckon with our assumptions about this connection with mounting urgency. Individual reactions to the environment are influenced not only by the sociocultural surround, but also by one’s internal world, particularly whether one’s internal objects are felt to have been irreparably damaged or are potentially salvageable. Excessive guilt about destructive impulses can limit one’s ability to recognize individual responsibility for the current situation without catastrophizing the weight of one’s ecological footprint. Delusional solutions may be adopted, valorizing extreme forms of engagement. The film First Reformed illustrates what appear to be divergent approaches to the planet: ruthless exploitation by an industrialist versus an impassioned objection to destruction of the environment for economic gain. In this narrative, both are underpinned by a paranoid-schizoid mindset. We investigate the dynamics of this psychic state, which results in a constricted perception and an absence of constructive agency with respect to the environmental crisis. We present an alternative conceptualization of the natural world’s place within a person’s psychic equilibrium. We emphasize that a more integrated relationship with the environment can facilitate concerned, preservative action on behalf of the natural world and ourselves.

responseSet.title: “Truth shall Spring Out of the Earth…”: The Analyst as Gatherer of Sense Impressions
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Truth shall Spring Out of the Earth…1”: The Analyst as Gatherer of Sense Impressions1

Avner Bergstein

Avner Bergstein

avnerbergstein@gmail.com
14, Nahum Street, Ramat Gan, Israel 5223344
+972 54 4287674
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-2301-5902

Avner Bergstein  

Bion, intuition, unrepressed unconscious

In this paper, the author reflects on the analyst's attempt to approach the unknowable, ultimate reality of the psychoanalytic experience, and the unrepressed unconscious. Drawing on both Freud's conception of ‘attention’ and Bion's emphasis on the power of observation, the paper explores the clinical implications of the notion of intuition as a primary tool in this endeavour. Intuition is often associated with Bion's thinking about the apprehension of psychic reality. At the same time, Bion's emphasis on the need to suspend sensuous aspects of reality on the way to encountering non-sensuous experience has given rise to much confusion. With the aid of detailed clinical material, the paper portrays intuition as a paradoxical, receptive state of mind oriented towards invariant, ultimate reality, and yet perpetually flickering between both sensuous and psychic aspects of reality. Moreover, since emotional truth is always in flux, intuition is seen as a capacity to apprehend reality in transience. In following the path of Bion's evolving thinking about the analyst's intuition, the author illustrates the continuity between the so-called ‘early Bion’ and the one referred to as ‘late Bion’.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and the Community - Introductory Considerations

Georg Bruns and James Barron

James Barron

gj.bruns@t-online.de
Bremen, Germany

Boston, MA, USA

James Barron  

The widening of the field of psychoanalysis toward enquiry of the social dimension of human existence is not limited to theory, but it also implies an expansion of our ability to conduct therapeutic interventions in diverse human situations.

(Tubert-Oklander 2014).

Community psychoanalysis has deep historical roots.1 Using PEP-Web to search for psychoanalysis in the community yields 1856 results, beginning with the American psychiatrist and psychoanalyst Trigant Burrow (1914)2 who conceptualized psychoanalysis as a social science, coined the term “group therapy” and stressed the communal aspects of psychic functioning. Despite the ongoing contributions to the literature, including the burgeoning of articles on community psychoanalysis in the 2000s, there is a resistance in our field to incorporating community psychoanalysis into what we consider as the mainstream of psychoanalytic theory and practice, and into our programmes of psychoanalytic training and education. In the USA one pioneering exception has been the Community Psychoanalysis Track at the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California (PINC), an IPA-accredited institute, and the affiliated Community Psychoanalysis Consortium (Chow and St. John 2021; González and Peltz 2021).

The need for this Section in the IJP emerged from our collective recognition that there is a growing number of papers focused on community psychoanalysis, but no dedicated intellectual home for those papers in this journal. Our initial intention was to create a single Special Issue with that focus. Our Call for Papers elicited 57 submissions from 15 countries on a wide range of topics such as the relevance and applicability of psychoanalytic perspectives to engaging with survivors of sexual trafficking in the USA, reflecting on racist violence and colonialism in Australia, working with refugees and asylum seekers from Central America, and understanding the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on staff working in mental health services in the UK. This robust response confirmed the widespread interest in contributing to the evolution of the theories and practices of psychoanalysts working with a variety of individuals, groups and systems in community settings and organizations. We also observed some recurring challenges that some authors encountered. While they invariably demonstrated a commitment to social justice and working with marginalized or underserved populations, they sometimes failed to describe

responseSet.title: The Case of T: Working as an Analyst with Trauma and its Consequences
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Case of T: Working as an Analyst with Trauma and its Consequences

Heinz Weiss

Heinz Weiss

Heinz Weiss  

In this presentation, I will describe clinical material from a traumatized patient, Mr. T., whose claustro-agoraphobic anxieties and concrete thinking confronted me with considerable difficulties. This led us repeatedly to situations where we seemed to talk at cross-purposes.

In those moments, there seemed to be no space for either of us and although I tried to maintain an observing stance, we sometimes got into reproachful confrontations. This was often the case during the first three years of the analysis and led to repetitive cycles where it seemed that each of us would blame the other and demand that he change.

There was some evidence that Mr. T had undergone considerable trauma during his childhood and youth, which had let to several breakdowns in the past.

I will start with Mr. T’s history, then shortly summarize the course of his treatment, describe the overall atmosphere that prevailed in the first years and eventually present a session in more detail. This kind of session was typical for this phase of his analysis (third year, four sessions weekly). I will go on to describe the gradual change of atmosphere during the fourth and fifth year and present a session from this period, which, I think, illustrates the difficulties in communication we found ourselves still confronted with.

Clinical Material

Mr. T. is a 40-year-old IT-assistant. When he came to see me he was in was depressed fearing he could become mad. As he told me in the assessment interview, he had been in analytic treatment already several times. However, all these treatments had ended in a deadlock and eventually been broken off, because, as he put it, the therapists could no longer ‘stand’ him. He withheld information on parts of his history, in particular a longer psychotic breakdown in his early twenties. He was afraid that, like his previous therapists, I would find him ‘intolerable’ and if I terminated his analysis, he said, nobody would accept him anymore which meant that he would be exposed to loneliness, madness or death.

responseSet.title: The Maternal Covenant Role and Function of the Mother in the Social Field
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Maternal Covenant Role and Function of the Mother in the Social Field

Lionel Bailly

Lionel Bailly

l.bailly@ucl.ac.uk
London, UK

Lionel Bailly  

Maternal Covenant; Maternal Metonymy; siblings; Law of the mother

Juliet Mitchell suggested the need for a principle to intervene at the level of sibling murderousness and called it the law of the mother in reference to Jacques Lacan’s law of the father. While binding the mother and the child to an agreement, this principle does not possess all the properties of a Law. This type of formal agreement has existed in legal thinking since the highest antiquity and is called a covenant. The covenant is not universal, it is valid only for the two parties who commit to it and its transgression is not penalised by a third party representative of the Other. I have called this regulatory principle the Maternal Covenant. It’s power rests on a metonymic process, in which the mother invokes kinship while hiding the unconscious object of her desire. I call this process the Maternal Metonymy. The concepts of Maternal Covenant and Maternal Metonymy are useful tools for the understanding and treatment of issues such as the pathological use of violence, unbearable rivalry both within the family and in the social group, failure or dysfunctional socialisation and the consequences of indecent or shameful behaviours.

responseSet.title: The Infantile, A Living Force
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Infantile, A Living Force

Virginia Ungar

Virginia Ungar

virginiaungar@gmail.com

Virginia Ungar  

It is indeed a great pleasure for me to pen a response to Adela Abella’s Letter to the Editor entitled “The infantile: More than Hilflosigkeit?” The letter is a deep and at the same time open reflection on the theme of the 52nd Congress of the IPA, the title of which was “The Infantile”, and the debates that took place during the first online meeting of the IPA in the last two weeks of July this year.

The text addresses directly the discussions we had choosing the theme of the Congress, especially as one of the first issues that came up was that in some languages - English, for example - the word “infantile” has a negative connotation pointing as it can to, for instance, immature behaviour. Moreover, there seem to have been differences over the translation of the term “infantile”, and both issues are addressed by Adela in her letter. Taking all of the considerations into account, however, we came to the conclusion that the concept has different meanings in different cultures and that this questioning could itself form part of the debate to take place.

But let me say something about how we came to choose this topic for the congress in the first place. On the one hand, the choice followed on from “The Feminine”, the topic of the 2019 Congress in London, in the sense that it was an attempt to stimulate the process of conceptualization of the terminology that we psychoanalysts use in our debates and publications, something that often happens while ignoring the cloud of meanings which surround each term.

On the other hand, we came to the conclusion that the linguistic questions linked to “the infantile” should form part of the debate at the heart of the congress as those questions appeared to go beyond the semantic, opening up other paths of investigation and discussion.

Yet another reason for the choice of the title was that, as a child analyst, I firmly believe that analytic work with children makes an invaluable contribution to psychoanalytic work with patients of all ages. In my time in the IPA, I was the Chair of the Integrated Training Committee from 2009 to 2015 when approval was given for the possibility for every Institute or Component Organization to offer an Integrated Training Program under the definition that forms the basis of such a programme: an additional and optional track for IPA training, in order to become a recognized adult, child and adolescent analyst.

responseSet.title: Transgenerational Violence and Immunological Deficits, a Psychosomatic Hypothesis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transgenerational Violence and Immunological Deficits, a Psychosomatic Hypothesis

Berdj Papazian

Berdj Papazian

bpapaz@bluewin.ch
Bernex, Switzerland

Berdj Papazian  

Somatizing identification, narcissism, aggression, immunodeficiency, transgenerational, epigenetics

This essay relies on the somatising identification mechanism, conceived to account for the delayed effects of projective identifications on the body, in this case by immunodeficiency. Three clinical cases with a similar dynamic will lead the author to deduce the mechanisms at work in the double operation of transduction from the psychic to the somatic, and transmission from one generation to the next. In each case, borderline mothers have suffered from their father's lack of interest and seem to be using their daughter to take revenge narcissistically. Defenceless, the latter grow in a state of resignation and it is their own daughters who become sometimes bubble babies, sometimes neutropenic or who develops juvenile arthritis. The initial obstacle to oedipal structuring appears as the trigger of these transgenerational pathologies whose somatic outcome would be the return of a denied symbolic castration. Destructive aggression is the common thread that most likely involves epigenetic modifications. To comprehend this type of alteration happening at the passage from psychic to somatic, a splitting of the primordial self is postulated at the undifferentiated level of the protomental. It is from this originary intersection, contained in a pre-object transitionality, that psychic and somatic paths are initiated. Bion and de M'Uzan serve as main references, as well as Anzieu and Green.

[[RunningHead]]

“Reappraising John O. Wisdom’s Critique of W.R. Bion’s Learning from Experience at a Meeting of the British Psychoanalytical Society—17 October 1964”

Joseph Aguayo

Joseph Aguayo

joseph.aguayo@gmail.com
11849 West Olympic Blvd., Suite 202 Los Angeles, CA 90064, USA

11849 West Olympic Blvd., Suite 202 Los Angeles, CA 90064, USA

London, UK.

Joseph Aguayo  

History of psychoanalysis, W, R, Bion theories, J, O, Wisdom, archival research

The paper takes up Wisdom's (1964) little known but significant critique of Bion's book, Learning From Experience. While Wisdom appreciates Bion's clinical and theoretical innovations, he criticised the at times confusing structure of themes that were not sufficiently segregated, the extraneous use of mathematical notation, and the scientific/deductive part of Bion's monograph. In deploying a crucial distinction from Wisdom's critique, one that separates the scientific/deductive aspect of Bion's work from its clinical/inductive trajectory, the author offers an approach to reading Bion's complex text.

responseSet.title: Response to Abram’s “On Winnicott’s Concept of Trauma”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Abram’s “On Winnicott’s Concept of Trauma”

Nathan Szajnberg

Nathan Szajnberg

nmoshe@gmail.com
3941 Duncan P l., Palo Alto, CA 94306, USA

Nathan Szajnberg  

Dear Editor:

Jan Abram’s “On Winnicott’s Concept of Trauma” and the elegant overview offered by Heinz Weiss (IJP Volume 102, Number 4, pp. 778-793, 755-764) offer clarifying details on Winnicott’s clinical (including pediatric and war-time service) observations on his concept of “trauma”, a term that can be overused and diffuse. Abram demonstrates that Winnicott’s concept focusing on infantile/early childhood trauma connects early “parent-infant psychic transmissions”, “because of the mother’s affective states … ” and that earlier trauma can result in “more severe outcomes”. Yet Winnicott also subtly argues that there must be some psychic structure available in the infant in order to “experience” what to the outside observer may be maternal (or paternal) traumatogenic acts. This letter complements and adds conceptual, empirical and developmental foundations to Abram’s Winnicott observations on trauma in early childhood. The first contribution is from John Bowlby, Winnicott’s contemporary at the Tavistock. The second, some three decades later, is from Dan Stern. Both researcher/clinicians, while working from somewhat different theoretical frames, strengthen and further detail developmentally Winnicott’s ideas on trauma.

Bowlby (1969) introduced “attachment” as that construction between infant and parent that, during times of fear, results in safety and comforting and, in the infant, in the balance of security and exploration. This adds a conceptual and developmental framework that complements and enriches psychoanalytic theory and Winnicott’s ideas in general. Specifically, Bowlby’s work defines “trauma” in early childhood at least as any serious loss or threatened loss of a major caregiving figure. This can be the death of a parent, or severe psychosis (such as Green’s dead mother), severe drug addiction and possibly also divorce, as shown empirically in our 30-year developmental study (Massie and Szajnberg [2008] 2021). Ainsworth et al. (1978) were able to demonstrate developmental stages and specific infant “communications” (the Strange Situation that is the sensorimotor acts of the eight- to twelve-month-old infant) that reveal the infant’s view of their attachment to specific caregivers (mother, father or metapellet). By 18 months of age, the Strange Situation is outmoded by the child’s cognitive development, and more developmentally appropriate measures supersede these (Szajnberg 2013; Waters and Deane 1985). That is, through attachment, Bowlby offers us more developmentally detailed, empirically confirmed observations to enrich Winnicott’s views of trauma. In fact, even the concept of infantile trauma is reconceptualized by Bowlby - as real

CONTACT Nathan Moses Szajnberg nmoshe@gmail.com Israel and San Francisco Societies, 3941 Duncan P l.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Abram’s Response to the Letter to Editor of IJP

Jan Abram

Jan Abram

janabram3@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-8776-9036

Jan Abram  

30th November 2021

Dear Editor,

The aim of my paper “On Winnicott’s Concept of Trauma” (commissioned by the Education Section of the IJP), was to examine and outline Winnicott’s writings on intrapsychic trauma. My examination resulted in further clarity, at least for me, that Winnicott’s focus on the mother’s psychic states of mind towards her infant makes all the difference to whether or not the infant will be protected from intrapsychic trauma at the start of life. This focus, as I suggested, means that Winnicott’s concept of trauma diverges from both Freud’s and Klein’s theories of anxiety. This divergence is one of the aspects of Winnicott’s theoretical matrix that, as I have argued elsewhere, advances psychoanalytic theory and practice (Abram 2013).

The authors of the letter suggest that there are several theories that complement Winnicott’s concept of trauma and they cite well-known formulations from the work of Bowlby, Stern and others. They also suggest that my paper on Winnicott would be “enriched” by including these theories. While I can see that the cited examples are certainly interesting, complementary observations that could be seen to enhance Winnicott’s concept of trauma, I am left questioning whether it is possible to integrate these theories into the fabric of Winnicott’s clinical paradigm. My question relates to the compatibility of different epistemologies that inevitably drive different types of research, i.e. the difference between evidence-based/observational research vs the Freudian single-case study research, to examine human development.

Winnicott’s clinical paradigm emanates not so much from his “observations” of the behaviour of the families he worked with in his role as a paediatrician but rather, and fundamentally, from his experience of being in analysis and becoming a psychoanalyst of children and adults (Abram [1996] 2007). What mostly interested Winnicott was to detail the ineffable connections between the intrapsychic, the interpsychic and the interpersonal that he saw as a lifelong struggle for the psyche (Winnicott 1971).

responseSet.title: In Memory of Eglé Laufer (1925-2021)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In Memory of Eglé Laufer (1925-2021)

François Ladame

François Ladame

Geneva
Francois.Ladame@unige.ch

François Ladame  

Eglé Laufer was born in Vienna in 1925 and passed away in London in 2021. She was a Distinguished Fellow of the British Psychoanalytic Society and Founder and President of the Brent Centre for Young People. Eglé had moved to London with her mother and her stepfather when she was seven years old; her mother was a child psychiatrist and her stepfather a mathematician. Eglé initially gained a degree in mathematics and physics but later decided to train as a psychoanalyst.

I had the chance to enjoy a nearly fifty-year-long affectionate relationship with Eglé. It started with my first visit in 1973 to the Brent Centre for Young People in London, which she had founded a few years before with her husband, Moses Laufer, and a few other psychoanalysts committed to treating severely ill adolescent patients. This visit was the beginning of my ongoing relationship with both Eglé and Moses Laufer although, after 1999, when Moe’s health deteriorated so badly, we had to go on with two instead of three. The Laufers visited my psychiatric outpatient clinic for adolescents in Geneva many, many times. As they were involved in the training programme in child, adolescent and adult psychiatry, generations of trainees are indebted to them for their unique teaching and still feel grateful today for all that they have learned.

Before focusing on adolescence, Eglé started her professional experience with adult patients, mainly female patients, postpartum psychosis and the prevention of breakdown. Like her I was trained as an adult psychoanalyst and not as a child psychoanalyst, as Moses Laufer was. Over the years, Eglé remained deeply interested in femininity, the development of female sexuality and masturbation in female adolescents. However, most of her scientific work is a joint venture with her husband, i.e. with a double authorship: Eglé and Moses Laufer.

Everywhere in the world, for all those concerned with ill adolescents and adolescent psychopathology, reference will be made to the Laufers. Their input is theoretical - they offer an understanding of adolescent psychopathology which did not exist before - and practical: once a psychoanalyst has concluded that a developmental disorder is present in a given adolescent, the analyst cannot remain “neutral”; instead they have to be clear regarding the risks for the future and the need for treatment.

responseSet.title: A Look at Albert Einstein’s Question “Why War?” with a Focus on Large-Group Psychology
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Look at Albert Einstein’s Question “Why War?” with a Focus on Large-Group Psychology

Vamık Volkan

Vamık Volkan

Charlottesville, Virginia

Lenox, MA, USA

Vamık Volkan  

Large-group identity; depositing; chosen trauma; entitlement ideology; time collapse; political propaganda

There are observable realistic factors - economic, legal and political - that contribute to initiating and maintaining international conflicts, including starting wars. Behind such factors there are also various psychological elements. This paper recalls correspondence between Albert Einstein and Sigmund Freud and Freud’s pessimistic response to the question “Why War?” and explores this question from the point of view of large-group psychology. The term “large group” describes thousands or millions of people, most of whom never see or even know about each other as individuals, but who share many of the same sentiments and the same realistic or fantasied ancestral history. This paper defines a concept, “large-group identity”, and describes how protecting it plays a key role in maintaining and even initiating war-like situations or wars. This paper also considers the psychological make-up of a decision-maker with political power who encourages war.

[[RunningHead]]

Why War in the Age of the Anthropocene?: The Dialogue between Freud and Einstein and the Question of Peace

Jean-Claude Stoloff

Jean-Claude Stoloff

jc.stol@wanadoo.fr
Training analyst of the SPRF, 25 Bd Arago, 75013 Paris, France

Jean-Claude Stoloff  

War, Anthropocene, pandemia

In “Why War?,” Freud wonders whether a dictatorship of reason capable of countering war and the forces of Thanatos will be likely to impose itself. In the age of Anthropocene, the mass phenomena to which this pandemic gives rise shows that this favourable outcome is never definitively acquired, bringing some credence to Freudian skepticism.

responseSet.title: The Case of J: Returning to the Office
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Case of J: Returning to the Office

Abbot A. Bronstein

Abbot A. Bronstein

abbot.bronstein@gmail.com

Abbot A. Bronstein  

As a child, I remember my parents having read to me The Little Engine That Could,1(Piper 1930). Now, reading the same story to my grandchildren, I realise I had forgotten how in one sense it was tedious and repetitive but also contained wonderful drawings of trains, and animals, and circus performers. It was largely a story of how one needs lots of help. That trying to do things alone, though a part of growing up, can also be part of the arrogance of an omnipotent phantasy, both conscious and unconscious, that interferes with doing and accomplishing what at times feel like impossible tasks.

Working as an analyst during the last two years during the Covid pandemic has been, I believe, just that. Janet Malcolm (1982) wrote that our profession was an “impossible” one. She was both wrong and right. What truly makes it impossible is if you succumb to the arrogance of needing to do this endeavor alone. Without others such as colleagues, supervisors, friends and patients, we are forced to become analysts who must go it alone. The shielding over the last few years has left me, at least, finding the isolation within clinical work far more intense and troublesome than the known and desired analytic isolation of being with a patient lying on my couch in my consulting room.

Through the generosity of Dr Y, we are able to see her work with her patient J once again (Bronstein 2020). The Covid virus has taken a terrible toll on the physical and emotional well-being of many, and has caused psychoanalysts everywhere to shift the setting of their analysis; I see this as a move which was brought about by a kind of evolving terror and desperation on the part of both our patients and ourselves, as I mentioned in my first discussion of J. It was not a choice to work remotely, whether via telephone or zoom or another platform. It was forced upon us, not by distance but by a mutual fear.

The “Case of J” allows us to look at what has been the desired isolation, intimacy and privacy we used to have and which we have tentatively regained when we were able once more to see patients in person in analysis within our offices. Once again, Dr. Y has provided us with an example of how an analyst has had to struggle, as each of us have, with the forced isolation of working differently within the changed setting, yet still attempting to work as a psychoanalyst.
