responseSet.title: Letter From Moscow
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter From Moscow

Igor Kadyrov

Igor Kadyrov

imkadyrov@comtv.ru

Igor Kadyrov  

24th February: Attacks on Linking

As I write this letter, the shock and pain of “24th February”, which began with missile strikes on Ukraine, has yet to pass. We are learning about the tragic events - which, on President Putin's orders, changed the lives and work of our Ukrainian colleagues overnight - thanks to continued direct contact and some online meetings, including a meeting with the friends of the Ukrainian Psychoanalytical Society. Many of them had to flee with their families under fire and missile attack as the war zones reached their homes - which were appallingly destroyed, along with their towns - only to continue working in radically altered circumstances, or to rise in defence of their country. This whole time, it is impossible not to think about them, not to be filled with horror at what is happening to them and their loved ones, and also not to be astounded by their courage and resilience.

The focus of this letter is the current situation of psychoanalysis in Moscow, and more broadly in Russia, as I myself observe it, experience it and understand it. I must immediately emphasize that my letter is not written in the name of any particular society, institute or group of colleagues. I am exclusively expressing my own views, which may be subjective or selective, and may not coincide with the positions of my colleagues. I feel that I must emphasize this, so I am not leaving anybody open to attack, particularly in light of the rapidly changing Russian legislation of late, which has - in relation to the so-called “special military operation” in Ukraine - substantially curtailed freedom of speech in general and even the freedom to use certain words, such as “war”, “invasion” and so on.

In the Russian language, the word “peace” - as it was used in the era of Leo Tolstoy (even in the titles of various editions and tomes of his War and Peace) could be written with two different spellings and correspondingly has two meanings: (1) “peace” (“миръ”) as an absence of dispute or war; and (2) “the world” (“мiръ”), as in the universe: the Earth, the human race and/or society.1

responseSet.title: Introduction to ‘Working as an Analyst with Trauma’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to ‘Working as an Analyst with Trauma’

Abbot Bronstein

Abbot Bronstein

abbot.bronstein@gmail.com

Abbot Bronstein  

Trauma has been central to the theories of aeteology and the techniques of psychoanalysis since the beginnings of the movement. Almost no other aspect of psychoanalytic theory has been argued about as much as the theory of trauma and the techniques used to ‘undo’ or ‘work through’ traumatic events.

On the theory of the origins of hysteria, Freud and Breuer stated:

… the causal relation between the determining psychical trauma and the hysterical phenomenon is not of a kind implying that the trauma merely acts like an agent provocateur in releasing the symptom, which thereafter leads an independent existence. We must presume rather that the psychical trauma—or more precisely the memory of the trauma—acts like a foreign body which long after its entry must continue to be regarded as an agent that is still at work. (Breuer, J & Freud S, 1893)

As the theory evolved and shifted from the actual event of seduction to its phantasy, Freud added the complex and controversial notion of the death instinct, and the theory of repetition compulsion became central to the metapsychology and technique:

A condition has long been known and described which occurs after severe mechanical concussions, railway disasters and other accidents involving a risk to life; it has been given the name of ‘traumatic neurosis’. The terrible war which has just ended gave rise to a great number of illnesses of this kind. (Freud, S. 1920)

The question of why painful experiences are repeated has held sway through the evolution of analytic theory.

The work of Khan on cumulative trauma (1963), and Kohut on developmental traumas arising from failures of empathy, have led to intense arguments about theory, but more significantly, to controversial discussions about the techniques and goals of analytic work. Much of the discussion of unrepresented and unsymbolised states of mind is focused on early trauma and its impact on the mind’s ability to form and symbolise experiences. This has led to both creative and at times somewhat wild shifts in technique, which won’t easily allow for discussion within our field.

responseSet.title: Discussion: ‘The Case of T’ - Working as an Analyst with Trauma
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion: ‘The Case of T’ - Working as an Analyst with Trauma

Nancy Kulish PHD

Nancy Kulish

nkulish@aol.com

Nancy Kulish  

Heinz Weiss shares detailed process examples from selected analytic sessions some years apart. The analytic work with Mr. T, a traumatized man with claustro-agoraphobic anxieties and concrete thinking, was challenging: at times. Mr. T’s discourse was an impermeable stream of external details. He presented himself as a perpetual victim, mistreated and ill-used. His words could become penetrating missiles, things-in-themselves; his thinking, fragmented, confused. His narcissistic shell admitted no need for the analyst, no responsibility for his difficulties with others. In general, he pushed away the analyst and his attempts to understand. We recognize the long-lasting effects of trauma in his concrete and undifferentiated thinking, problems with affect regulation, an impoverished emotional life, and proneness to action. Analysts struggle with understanding the meanings and implications of these issues and trace them to the earliest stages of development. Many analysts argue that our usual technical approaches are inadequate - beyond interpretation - leaving us in need of modifications in technique, or looking for fresh ideas. Hence, we turn to containment, witnessing, transforming unverbalised thoughts into words, naming affects, empathic attunement, reconstruction of the past (See Boulanger, 2005; Greenberg, 2019; Krystal, 1978; Levine, 2014.)

Weiss, however, struggles to be able to think and to hold an analytic stance and to remain steadfast to his usual approach - a Kleinian one - of interpreting what he felt was going on in the patient’s mind (and his own) about the present situation between the two of them. He tells us of their mutual deadlock. I will return to this prolonged state of affairs, we might say enactment, or as Weiss says, “talking past each other,” where the analyst re-enactsthe role of a cruel, reproachful object trying to force critical comments into the patient's mind.

The consistent line Wiess takes in his interventions is his focus on aggression: the patient fears his own destructiveness, which he projects onto others, and he fears punishment for this. The transference/countertransference situation can be encapsulated by Mr. T’s long-standing conscious fantasy of being a rat imprisoned in a crib, threatened by knives and needing to bite back. This whole picture carries undertones of the patient’s early history in which the children were raised in a strict fundamentalist sect, forced to work like slaves, with a cold rejecting mother, and the death of a sister.

responseSet.title: Discussion of Heinz Weiss’s ‘The Case of T’ - Working with Trauma
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of Heinz Weiss’s ‘The Case of T’ - Working with Trauma

Priscilla Roth and Ignês Sodré

Ignês Sodré

Ignês Sodré  

As we consider the central theme of this fascinating clinical paper, the long analysis of a severely traumatised patient, we feel that our main task is to understand why the same situation repeats endlessly, with almost no chance of movement, and to explore how to conceptualise this in relation to the “incurability” of the trauma.

T had made several attempts at psychotherapy before he came to Heinz; these ended in failure. He has had a different experience with Heinz; analyst and patient are devoted to continuing their very difficult work, even though for a long time the conversation between them took place mostly in a negative atmosphere of confrontation, persecution, and stasis.

Patient and analyst were locked in despair, unable to get through to each other. Surrounded by an atmosphere of violence, caught up in a tragic, fundamental misunderstanding, each trying and trying, repeating and repeating, each feeling provoked, attacked, lonely and abandoned. Each felt defeated in his efforts to communicate; good efforts were met by provocation, projections of anger and guilt. The result was a mutual tragedy: analyst and patient were repetitively being re-traumatised.

We believe that such a repetition of the trauma is not only inevitable but is a necessary condition for the psychoanalytic treatment of patients suffering from traumatic experiences. It is the defining characteristic of psychoanalysis uniquely to be able to elicit, and eventually to understand, deeply believable confrontations within the analyst of the traumatic experiences of the patient. Heinz’s clinical paper demonstrates this in a convincing way.

T feels, indeed knows, that his survival depends on his analysis, even though his experience is often mostly of confusion, disappointment, and the invasion by dangerous projections. He continues to try to get through to the goodness of his object, which he seems to sense is there to be found, and which Heinz communicates to him through the quality of his own engagement and dedication. And, indeed, we are convinced that in this patient’s internal world, a primitive model/picture exists that allows the patient to imagine being understood and held, something that makes a positive transference possible; at the most primitive level, this is connected to an idealised object.

responseSet.title: How much Time does Psychoanalysis Take? The Duration of Psychoanalytic Treatments from Freud’s Cases to the Swedish Clinical Practice of Today
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

How much Time does Psychoanalysis Take? The Duration of Psychoanalytic Treatments from Freud’s Cases to the Swedish Clinical Practice of Today

Andrzej Werbart and Sven Lagerlöf

Sven Lagerlöf

andrzej@werbart.se
andrzej.werbart@psychology.su.se
Stockholm, Sweden
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-0859-1012

Stockholm SE-10691, Sweden

Sven Lagerlöf  

Clinical practice, training, duration, termination, Freud’s patients, survey

The starting point for this paper is Freud's thinking about the length of psychoanalysis and the conditions for ending it. His psychoanalytic treatments were intense, but varied greatly in length. Freud's assertion that deep psychological changes take time is still topical; however, the length of time has gradually increased. Available documentation from psychoanalytic training institutes, and findings from empirical studies, indicate that the mean length of psychoanalysis ranges from between three to seven years, and varies between different countries and periods. A recent survey among Swedish psychoanalysts found a mean length of 5.7 years and a wide variation in length from 1.5 to 12 years. Discussions in a seminar group on endings and the qualitative follow-up survey showed that a unique combination of factors determined the duration of psychoanalysis in each individual case. We briefly review the potential determinants of the treatment length and the different meanings of time in open-ended psychoanalytic treatments. To conclude, we stress the need for systematic clinical and empirical studies of determinants and the underlying processes behind the different lengths of psychoanalyses.

[[RunningHead]]

Sex, death and the superego: updating psychoanalytic experience and developments in neuroscience, by Ronald Britton, London, Routledge, 2021, This is the second edition of Sex, Death and the Superego: Experiences in Psychoanalysis (2003), £28.99, 151 pp.

Ignês Sodré

Ignês Sodré

isodre@hotmail.com

Ignês Sodré  

There are very good reasons to welcome the publication of the second edition of Ronald Britton’s Sex, Death and the Superego: it contains many changes and additions, large and small, and one completely new chapter, titled “PS(n+1) Brain, Mind and Self”, a development of Britton’s 2015 book Between Mind and Brain: Models of the Mind and Models in the Mind. To quote him on the subject of his neuroscientific observations (63): “I believe that some day the concept of the ego will be where the psychoanalytic theory of the mind and the neuro-scientific theory of the brain will meet. That is where Freud started”. About his concept of PS(n1) he says in his Introduction to the present edition:

I conceived of the term PS(n+1) from Bion’s PS< >D. … This seemed too static to me; in my version (n) denotes the current state of what is a lifetime of successive cycles of integration and de-integration. As life moves on and (n) increases in cycles of PS(n)>D(n), new knowledge means abandoning coherence, D(n), for a period of accumulation of facts and formulations that are un-integrated, what I call PS(n+1).

Britton also describes an important theoretical and clinical difference between the previous edition and this one: “I thought of myself as taking a new path, but I soon found out that I was stepping into the footsteps of an earlier pioneer, Ronald Fairbairn. As I have done, he stated that Freud’s system was based on redundant physics” (2). Fairbairn is mentioned only once in the first edition:

The idea that a part of the personality could be an enemy of the central self was described vividly by Fairbairn (1952). Initially he called this “the internal saboteur”, later changing this to “anti-libidinal ego” in a tripartite structure comprising a central ego and a libidinal and anti-libidinal ego. Fairbairn took the view from Melanie Klein that a primordial ego was engaged in object relations from the outset, and that splitting of the ego was its earliest defence. (118)

(Later Klein (1946) added Fairbairn’s use of the term “schizoid” in describing the paranoid-schizoid position.) The newly adopted term, the “internal saboteur”, which now permeates Britton’s theoretical/clinical descriptions in many of his chapters, implies, as he says, a sense of moving onto a new path, an important change in his thinking. You will find this new emphasis in his second paper on hysteria (37), and it is central to his discussion of one of Fairbairn cases of homosexuality in a woman (59). He also examines the relation between Fairbairn and Klein (69). The “internal saboteur” appears in his study of the relation between the ego and superego, and, most especially, in the third part of this book, on narcissism.

Masha Mimran

Masha Mimran

mashamimran@gmail.com
3rd Ave #201, New York, NY 10128

Masha Mimran  

Dear Candidate: Analysts from around the world offer personal reflections on psychoanalytic training, education, and the profession is a unique collection of letters from seasoned psychoanalysts, sharing both personal and professional reflections on their experiences as candidates. The 42 deeply personal letters, each addressing the “Dear Candidate”, are equally relevant to 918 psychoanalytic educators and inspiring to analysts at all levels. With each author discussing the complex feelings, anxieties, gratifications and frustrations that weave the fabric of their experience of psychoanalytic training, the reader is plunged into intimate and moving reminiscences of candidacy. Never dogmatic, the authors invite the anonymous addressee/candidate into a conversation, and in so doing, pioneer a dialogic thinking about the meanderings of training.

At first glance, the epistolary style brought to (my) mind Laclos's Dangerous liaisons. Indeed, in Dear Candidate, the reader finds the dramatic twists and turns of the candidate's life. Yet, this collection is first and foremost a work of deep generosity, care and thoughtful reflection, written with honesty, empathy, humour and love for our field.1 As importantly, the collection awaits answers from its reader - the candidate. This invitation to respond posits the “candidate” as the becoming analyst, both held in mind as the addressee, as well as present as an internal figure in the mind of the seasoned analyst. Most immediately, psychoanalysis is framed as a field that coalesces temporalities and generations, as well as processes of externalization and internalization. In some sense, the genre of the collected letters captures the meaning of becoming analyst insofar as Dear Candidate can be said to belong to the epistolary style only once the candidate writes a response.

Busch also “felt it wise to not put the letters in any categories or order, and therefore the letters are presented pretty much as I received them.” One of the reasons behind this choice, is Busch's belief that it was important “to intermingle letters from different geographical regions, to see if differences in psychoanalytic cultures could be observed” (xxiii). Busch's thoughtful arranging of the letters creates a conversation in plurality. The diversity of cultures (analytic and personal) both highlights and mirrors the already multiplied meanings that Dear Candidate shapes throughout the reading of the letters. Each letter further gives insight into the unfolding of a new story about training - in Chile, Argentina, France, the United States, Italy, Iran, Switzerland or Israel. In some instances, the candidate/reader is referred to as the “analyst in formation”, following European models of training such as the French model, and “analysts in training” in South America. One letter addresses two unknown candidates, Irina and Mark.

responseSet.title: A Metapsychological Framework for Sadomasochism
responseSet.authorMast: Jack Novick and  Kerry Kelly Novick
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Metapsychological Framework for Sadomasochism

Jack Novick and Kerry Kelly Novick

Kerry Kelly Novick

kerrynovick@gmail.com
283 Homans Avenue, Closter, NJ 07624 USA

Kerry Kelly Novick  

Sadomasochism, omnipotence, addiction, trauma, self-regulation

The authors present a summary of sadomasochism, its centrality in all pathology and the difficulties encountered by all analysts in working with sadomasochism. An integrative model is presented with determinants and manifestations from all phases of development, including the transmission of pathology between generations. Addiction to pain and the formation of hostile omnipotent beliefs are described as components of sadomasochism that explain how resistant sadomasochism is to change and growth. The emergent idea of two systems of self-regulation is presented and its utility for technique is described. Specific focus on developmental aspects offers therapists developmental markers they can use in clinical work.

responseSet.title: Self-Harm
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Self-Harm

Humberto Persano

Humberto Persano

hpersano@gmail.com
Malabia 2420 1st floor, Buenos Aires 1425, Argentina

Humberto Persano  

Self-harm, self-aggression, masochism, impulsivity, violence

This paper attempts to address self-harm from a psychoanalytic perspective in contemporary clinical practice and their relationship with aggression, impulsiveness, violence, and masochism. Self-harm is frequent in adolescents and young people, predominantly in feminine gender. The author describes the different manifestations of self-injurious behaviors in the contemporary clinic, its manifestation in adolescents and its relationship with psychopathology. Self-harm is a way of communication, through actions, to deal with psychic pain and with the self-vulnerability. Self-harm behaviors are a manifestation of psychic tension that results overwhelming, intolerable and its discharge relieves the subject suffering, although this way is ephemeral. Suffering is linked to childhood traumatic experiences, where the self of the infant was cathected with a greater amount of aggression than erotic libido. The author states that affective aggression is triggered by emotions like anger, sadness, and emptiness. Archaic masochism is a form of identification with the aggressor and a turn against oneself aggression commonly associated with episodes of child abuse. Also, it argues that the violence linked to self-injury is intersubjective fabric in nature and requires an interdisciplinary approach.

responseSet.title: The Work of Benno Rosenberg
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Work of Benno Rosenberg

Denys Ribas

Denys Ribas

33, rue Traversière, 75012, Paris. France
denysribas@free.fr

Denys Ribas  

Masochism, drive fusion, death drive, melancholia, psychosomatic

Benno Rosenberg's work has been little translated into English. Yet, his work on masochism is a landmark in France. He set himself the goal of deploying all the richness and implications of the second Freudian drive theory and the introduction of the death drive. He therefore returns to “the Economic Problem of Masochism” to give all its value to the drive fusion that it achieves internally and which is therefore for it “guardian of life”, even if it is can also be fatal. He draws consequences on the psychic construction of the ego, the superego and on that of temporality. He also describes a “work of melancholy” which is different from mourning. Thinking it necessary to push the consequences of the new theorizing further than Freud was able to do, he revisits “Inhibition, Symptom and Anxiety” to articulate the triggering of anxiety by a libidinal demand and the threat to the unity of the ego who comes from the death drive due to conflict. Starting from the psychosomatic descriptions of Pierre Marty, whose options are specified, he also proposed a metapsychology of somatization during the overflow of the psyche by the destructiveness which will then threaten the body.

[[RunningHead]]

Una vita cura una vita. Inizi, maturità, esiti d’una vocazione. Una biografia professionale,1 by Franco Borgogno, Turin, Bollati Boringhieri, 2020.

Giuseppe Civitarese

Giuseppe Civitarese

http://orcid.org/0000-0002-3466-4329
gcivitarese@gmail.com

Giuseppe Civitarese  

The Style and the Person

When I was asked to review the most recent book by Franco Borgogno (henceforth, B.) I initially felt a slight sense of unease because it seemed to me that there was no need for it. A review, like a preface or an introduction, usually has the function of interesting the reader in a text, easing their path towards it, suggesting a way in; overall, sparing them a bit of effort and helping them see why it is worth taking time to read the book. In B.'s case, I would say that the best review is the actual reading of the book. Like certain novels where the beginning grabs you by the throat and doesn't let you go, B. has a style that recommends itself. As we know, when something is good, the best criticism is tautology.

B.'s language is direct, expressive, everyday, ‘anarchic’, evocative, playful, ironic. I think he would go along with the suggestion that I often give to young colleagues who come to learn: talk as if they were at the bar, chatting with a friend (without “giving interpretations”). It's an effective trick for helping them shed an irritatingly jargon-heavy and lifeless way of speaking. No editor would give a moment's consideration to texts written in that kind of language. They would simply say that there's no voice. And besides, B. freely admits that he wrote the first draft of Chapter 4 at an outside table of Bar 900 near Cuneo while enjoying “cappuccinos with cocoa and croissants doused in butter and jam” (p. 11).

With his somewhat Falstaffian aura and impetuous manner, those who are acquainted with B. know him as someone warm and affectionate, infectious in his enthusiasm. You realize straight away that he has preserved a great capacity for astonishment. The world does not bore him; he likes being in the world and doing what he does. You listen to him talking in a delicate and persuasive tone and at the same you see the laughter in his eyes. He takes what he does extremely seriously but does not take himself too seriously. If I am dwelling on his portrait it's because the book is a personal biography; this makes it a text where the theory, technique and history of psychoanalysis and of its institutions are woven inextricably into his personal story. In B. we clearly see the huge importance of personality and temperament in determining what type of analyst you will be.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Body as Psychoanalytic Object: Clinical Applications from Winnicott to Bion and Beyond, edited by Caron Harrang, Drew Tillotson, and Nancy C. Winters, London & New York, Routledge, 2022, 246 pp., ISBN: 978-1-032-04915-1 (hbk).

Michael Robbins

Michael Robbins

mdrobbinsmd@comcast.net

Michael Robbins  

This edited collection of papers comes from the 12th annual British Object Relations Conference in Seattle in 2018. The papers focus primarily on the work of Winnicott, Bion and related object relations theorists, particularly Bion’s ideas about how mind makes meaning and thought out of primordial sensory-perceptual-somatic experience, and Winnicott’s ideas about the kind of undifferentiated maternal immersion and mirroring necessary to assist the infant in its primitive state to comprehend meaning itself. The authors attempt to apply these and other related concepts to their clinical experience with patients whose language does not express reflective thought but is dissociated from their basic experience and expression, which is somatic, affective and behavioural.

Providing unity, coherence and a sense of direction to an edited collection of papers is a problem. The editors have written introductions to each of its six sections; therefore 13 of the 19 chapters are original. Unfortunately, each chapter has a separate reference list, and the single index is relatively small. The undifferentiated, unintegrated, non-reflective somatic-behavioural mode that characterizes most of the patients described in the book is compatible with my understanding of psychosis, yet the index contains just four references to it, and none to the specific terms psychosis, psychotic or schizophrenic.

The authors are articulate, even poetic, and very knowledgeable on the aspects of object relations theory about which they write. The book explores a very important question; how can the analyst, functioning in reflective thought and language, relate in a mutative way to patients who function primarily in a non-reflective, concrete, enactive and somatic-affective mode. Most of the patients the authors describe are unable to think reflectively and do not suffer in the classical psychoanalytic sense of neurotic repression of mind in unconscious conflict. The language in which they understand themselves and relate to others is dissociated and abstracted from their somatic- affective core. Even if the sensitive analyst establishes an empathic resonance with such persons, how are they to communicate with them in a way that might be mutative using the analytic tool of language? Whatever overall impression one might come away with from reading the book - and mine was mixed - it will challenge and inspire the reader to think deeply about this important question.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bion 365 Quotes by Nicola Abel-Hirsch, Routledge, London and New York.

Lawrence Brown

Lawrence Brown

drlawrencejbrown@gmail.com

Lawrence Brown  

The writings of Wilfred Bion have been studied since his early papers on group process in the mid-1940s through the early1950s, and were followed by his investigation of psychotic processes in the mid-1950s. His work took another turn at the beginning of the 1960s with the 1136 publication of an important paper, The Psychoanalytic Study of Thinking (1962) which anticipated and signaled his move into a new area of interest that began with Learning from Experience (1962), the first of a quartet in the collection Seven Servants (1977). Learning was followed by three further books: Elements of Psycho-Analysis (1963), which extended the concepts of alpha and beta elements and alpha function introduced in Learning.

The third book, Transformations (1965), which I consider his magnum opus, is subtitled “Change from Learning to Growth”, which links this book to Learning from Experience (1962) and also underscores Bion’s shift in focus to mental growth and the processes that occur between analysand and analyst to promote or forestall psychic development. His deeper discussion of such concepts as O, the “Grid” and his statement that “The differentiation I wish to introduce is not between conscious and unconscious, but between finite and infinite” (46), and that the analyst should train his attention to what is unknowable - O (or the Truth) - provoked O’Shaughnessy’s concern that Bion’s “late thinking becomes less boundaried … [that] make the texts too open, too pro- and e-vocative” (O'Shaughnessy, E., 2005, Who's Bion?. IJP, 86: 1523-1528). In the fourth book, Attention and Interpretation (1970), written after his move to Los Angeles, Bion furthered his application of some religious notions meant as metaphors that he introduced at the end of Transformations, but that have been criticized as his having taken a mystical turn.

Bion’s oeuvre consists of far more than these four foundational texts that have generated a veritable industry of Bionian-influenced papers, books, study groups and local, national and international conferences. It is my understanding that after Freud, Bion is the most quoted psychoanalyst of our time. Centres of Bionian thinking have arisen across the globe in London, Los Angeles and San Francisco, Boston, Pavia (Italy) and numerous South American cities, especially Rio de Janeiro, Sao Paulo and Porto Allegre. Many of his concepts are difficult to penetrate, such as the following quote from Attention and Interpretation: “The analytic situation requires greater width and depth that can be provided by a model from Euclidean space” (Collected Works, VI, 2014, pp. 114-115), a perplexing assertion that requires a knowledge of Euclidean geometry and also a sense of how the analytic session may be conceived as linked to mathematics.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bion and Primitive Mental States: Trauma and the Symbolic Link, 1st Edition, by Judy K. Eekhoff, New York, Routledge, 2021, 184pp., $39.95, IBSN-13 978-1032149097

Robert White

Robert White

robert.s.white@yale.edu

Robert White  

Judy Eekhoff has provided us with a personal exploration into the difficult and sometimes impossible psychotherapy of patients with primitive mental disorders. I say personal as each of us interested in working with these patients must find our own way of working. How can we make contact? What theoretical language do we find useful? How do we deal with the relentlessness of countertransference pressure and enactment? How do we find at-one-ment with terror, death and negativity? These are the questions that Eekhoff takes up and addresses in her own journey, her own way of working. But this is not a textbook; it cannot and should not be. What is useful about such a book is that it is one possible path to creative ideas on how to think about and work with such patients. Each of us would take a different way based on our personal countertransference, personal way of listening and unique mix of theory. Grotstein (2009) calls this “sharing personal recipes” (5). I will try to show in this review where my sensibilities and those of Eekhoff may overlap in some general principles. We can learn much from one another's creativity and innovation.

What is this book not? It does not present a unified theory. Eekhoff draws heavily on the theories of trauma, the theoretical writing of Bion and, to a lesser degree, theories from Ferenczi, Klein, Winnicott, Bleger, Meltzer, Grotstein and others. First, I suggest we have three different languages here, a psychiatric trauma language, and two distinct psychoanalytic languages: Freudian and Kleinian/Bionian. These languages are not compatible, although it is possible to form bridges among them. In the book, these different languages and concepts derived from them are not delineated and it is up to the reader to know their origins. I think this book would only be useful if someone already had a working knowledge of Klein and Bion, and a familiarity with recent work on unrepresented states; it would be hard to follow without such knowledge.

responseSet.title: Roger Money-Kyrle’s 1934 Paper on War: The Context and Personal Background
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Roger Money-Kyrle’s 1934 Paper on War: The Context and Personal Background

Heinz Weiss

Heinz Weiss

Auerbachstraße 110, D-70376 Stuttgart
(heinz.weiss@rbk.de)

Myliusstraße 20, D-60323 Frankfurt a.M.
(weiss@sigmund-freud-institut.de)

Heinz Weiss  

Roger Money-Kyrle; war; propaganda; paranoid fears; manic defences; reparative processes; restitutive functions

This brief introduction summarizes and gives the biographical context of Roger Money-Kyrle’s 1934 paper “A psychological analysis of the causes of war”. It also contextualizes his interest in the interplay between the psychological mechanisms operating within the individual, the society and groups and his personal experience with Germany in between and after two catastrophic wars.

responseSet.title: Psychological Roots of the Climate Crisis: Neoliberal Exceptionalism and the Culture of Uncare, by Sally Weintrobe, London, Bloomsbury, 2021, 332 pp, £21.78, ISBN: 978-1-5013-7286-5
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychological Roots of the Climate Crisis: Neoliberal Exceptionalism and the Culture of Uncare, by Sally Weintrobe, London, Bloomsbury, 2021, 332 pp, £21.78, ISBN: 978-1-5013-7286-5

David Black

David Black

dmblack@clara.co.uk
London, UK

David Black  

Sally Weintrobe is doing an important thing in writing this book. She is taking the insights of modern psychoanalysis, and finding a popular style in which to apply them to one of the central issues, perhaps the central issue, of our times. There are undoubtedly hazards to doing this, above all the danger of excessive simplification of either the psychoanalytic ideas or the public issues, but unless we can find ways to do it there is the companion risk that psychoanalytic ideas, much needed in the wider world, will remain stuck in a professional silo.

Weintrobe is a well-respected figure in the world of climate change discussion. Her earlier (edited) book, Engaging with Climate Change, made a powerful impact when it appeared in 2014, and was shortlisted for the International Gradiva Prize for contributions to psychoanalysis (the title of which reminds us that Freud too felt strongly that psychoanalysis had an important contribution to make beyond the consulting room). Since then she has written and spoken widely and in many contexts on the subject of climate change and ecocide: the present book is the distillation of a great deal of thinking.

It is nowhere so powerfully distilled as on page 57, where we meet the distillation of the distillation. Speaking of James Hansen’s report on climate change to the US Congress in 1988, Weintrobe writes: “Its main import was that humanity was tasked with growing up psychologically.” That in a nutshell is the message the climate emergency conveys: if humanity cannot grow up psychologically, if we, the human population of the Earth, are unable to recognize the adult truths of interdependence and interconnection, our infantile responses will destroy us.

The central puzzle of “climate change” is that the issue is so obvious and yet so little has been done to address it. I remember my father, a soil research chemist, telling me in the 1950s of what he called the “greenhouse effect”, adding as a corollary: “and if we continue to industrialize, we will heat up the planet”. Of course we would! That’s what “greenhouse gases” do. What he could not foresee, of course, was that the world’s human population would multiply by a factor of three over the ensuing 70 years, or that industrialization would spread across the entire globe, vastly increasing humanity’s demand on the Earth’s resources. But you might think, the science being so obvious, that as these facts became apparent, sensible governments and, still more, sensible economists would adapt to take them into account.

responseSet.title: The Mirror Operator: On Lacanian Logic
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Mirror Operator

On Lacanian Logic

Marc Heimann

Marc Heimann

marc.heimann@protonmail.com
Hochschule Niederrhein, FB 06 - Social Medicine/Social Psychiatry, Krefeld, Germany
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3757-1790

Marc Heimann  

Mirror, logic, identity, Lacan, theory

The paper explores the Lacanian metaphor of the mirror as a logical operator. While the mirror-stage has often been discussed regarding its imaginary relevance, the mirror as a logical operator has not yet been discussed. To mark a first approach to this, the paper discusses the formulas Lacan offers in his Seminar X, “Anxiety,” as an example for the use of this operator. The formulas show a great deal of complexity that binds several elements of Lacanian thought together. Within Lacanian theory, they show a distinct relationship to the specularised and non-specularised images of the Möbius strip, as well as to the cross-cap, and they offer a deeper insight into the widely used metaphor of the mirror. These formulas also offer a wider insight into how ‘mirroring’ can be understood formally in non-Lacanian psychoanalysis. The mirror operator as a logical tool enables analysts to conceive of how an indeterminate element is part of our identity and how this structures angst.

responseSet.title: Thinking About Encountering Masochism/Masochistic Elements in Analytic Practice in the Kleinian Tradition then and Now
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thinking About Encountering Masochism/Masochistic Elements in Analytic Practice in the Kleinian Tradition then and Now

Claudia Frank

Claudia Frank

Stuttgart, Germany
cl.frank@t-online.de

Claudia Frank  

Masochism, evil principle, countertransference issues

Masochistic elements are puzzling entanglements for most of us in the beginning. Taking Melanie Klein's attempts to think about them thus show one way how to make sense of them. Starting from her struggles with masochistic symptoms in child analyses in the early 1920ies Melanie Klein tried to conceptualize those experiences in a way that could help to fact them in the analytic process. In this article the development is outlined, the move from thinking about them in libidinal terms to gradually grasping in greater depth their destructive quality. She speaks of an “evil principle” and then finds a conclusive solution in conceptualizing masochism as a representation of the death drive. In the next decades her followers would further work on the complexities of the countertransference issues and superego organizations in masochism in more detail as will be shown. Finally, a look in a piece of present analytic work will exemplify the clinical usefulness of the Kleinian conceptualization.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Grammar of Paradox: Deciphering Winnicott's Language Theory

Ronnie Carmeli

Ronnie Carmeli

rondisk@gmail.com
5 Asher Barash St., Tel Aviv, Israel

Ronnie Carmeli  

Winnicott; Wittgenstein; potential space; language theory; grammar

This paper illuminates how the psychoanalytic work of Winnicott and Wittgenstein's philosophy on language complement each other in elucidating a “psychoanalytic language game”. The paper takes a close look at the grammar and linguistic mechanisms which underlie psychoanalytic work. Wittgenstein's linguistic turn from positivism is discussed; his ideas like ‘family resemblance’, ‘aspect seeing’ and ‘language games’ are examined in order to shed light on the paradoxical communication at play in Winnicott's potential space. The paper looks at the potentiality central to therapeutic work, arguing that it accords with what Wittgenstein calls the grammatical optative mode, as Freud already indicated in his theory of dreaming.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis in the Community in France
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis in the Community in France

Vassilis Kapsambelis

Vassilis Kapsambelis

Kapsambelis@wanadoo.fr
40 boulevard du Montparnasse, 75015, Paris, France

Vassilis Kapsambelis  

Mental health, community care, psychoanalysis, France

There has been a long tradition of psychoanalysis in the community in France. The first psychoanalysts working in public institutions did so in places such as the network of the Offices publics d’Hygiène sociale (OPHS), initially intended to treat tuberculosis, infant mortality, etc., after the Second World War. The introduction of psychiatric care in the community mobilized a genuine interpenetration of psychoanalysis and psychiatry. In 1953, the SPP founded the Centre de Consultations et de Traitements Psychanalytiques, and in 1955 Victor Smirnoff created a children's centre. In 1958, Philippe Paumelle created the Association de Santé mentale du 13ème arrondissement de Paris (ASM 13), a pilot project, implemented in 1960 as “district psychiatry”, which is France’s national policy of community psychiatry. In the consultation centres of these psychiatric districts, called centres médico-psychologiques (CMP), psychotherapy is provided by clinical psychologists, who are largely psychoanalytic in orientation. In 1972, Pierre Marty and Michel Fain created what would become the Institut de Psychosomatique in 1978. In 1974, ASM 13 created its own Centre de psychanalyse with Jean and Évelyne Kestemberg, specialising in psychotic and borderline pathologies. The activities and theoretical contributions of these psychoanalytic public centres are presented in this paper, with attention to: the distinction between treatment and consultation; the introduction of a new approach to psychosomatics; the description of a psychotic relation to the object as fetish; the research about free treatment and its implications. Currently, the situation of psychoanalysis in France is divided between the medical authorities who reject psychoanalysis in the name of evidence-based medicine, and the persistence of psychoanalysis in both public and private practice. More generally, it appears that the central issue at stake is the conception of the ‘psychic human being’ and psychic causality in Western societies.

Maternal Envy as Legacy: Search for the Unknown Lost Maternal Object

Jill Salberg

Jill Salberg

Jillsalberg@gmail.com
155 West 71st. Street Suite 1D, New York, NY 10023, USA
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-8071-3657

New York, USA

Jill Salberg  

Trans-generational transmissions; attachment trauma; envy; shame

The impact of intergenerational transmissions of trauma and the dissociative states of mind that cross from parents to their children has become an important expansion of psychoanalytic theory. Clinical material will be discussed showing how an early death of a mother haunted the lives of many generations of mothers and daughters. Considerations of attachment rupture, trauma, envy, deadly and deadening aggression and shame are discussed as part of transgenerational transmission phenomena and how they are worked on in the analytic relationship. Envious attacks, though painful to tolerate, nonetheless need to be processed in order to transform transmissions from the past.

responseSet.title: Editorial: Farewell
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial: Farewell

Dana Birksted-Breen

Dana Birksted-Breen

Dana Birksted-Breen  

You who read me, are you sure of understanding my language.

(Jorge Borges, 2000)

It has been a huge privilege to have held the editorship of this prestigious journal, established under the directorship of Freud, and a particular honour to be the first woman editor of the IJP. But above all, it has been a great pleasure to work with wonderful colleagues.

The challenge for the IJP has always been to uphold the essential principles of psychoanalysis while recognizing and engaging with important developments in the field. From its beginnings, psychoanalysis has faced attacks, its practice has been easily eroded. Ernest Jones pointed out that the more pernicious attack is the one that comes from the inside, when the ideas of psychoanalysis are accepted but “their meaning diluted until it [psychoanalysis] may be regarded as ‘harmless’” (Jones 1920, 4).

The opposition, Jones wrote, comes in the form of:

various seductive catchwords that appeal to attitudes or principles entirely legitimate in themselves, such as ‘resistance to dogma,’ ‘freedom of thought,’ ‘widening of vision,’ ‘re-adjustment of perspective,’ and so on. That this opposition may not only be displayed by outside antagonists but may assume subtle forms also amongst those having a nearer acquaintance with the subject. (Jones 1920, 4)

As the field of psychoanalysis grows, how do we distinguish between what is genuine development and what is defensive? The debates and exchanges around this question are ongoing, and the careful study of clinical material can further the conversation, although we cannot ever expect resolution when discussing unconscious forces.

Some general agreement recognizes significant developments in psychoanalysis, such as a greater exploration of the earliest time of life - that “Minoan-Mycenaean” epoch that Freud anticipated in women (Freud 1932, 282), or even an exploration of prenatal life and the caesura of birth, as well as intergenerational transmission. Recent developments have included increasing interest in the exploration of the somatic expression of what the psyche cannot tolerate, and of the difficulty of working with patients who cannot mentalize.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Reflections on the Conditions of Possibility of Human Destructiveness

David Bell

David Bell

London, UK
davidlbell@hotmail.com Flat 4 Mullion Court, 112-114 Finchley Road, London, NW3 5JH

David Bell  

War; psychoanalysis; Freud; Klein

This paper explores the psychoanalytic contribution to the understanding of war. It takes as its frame of reference the conditions of possibility in the human subject that form the basis for the detonation of such extremes of destruction. Starting with key papers of Freud that address this malaise of our “civilization” it goes on to consider the contributions from the Kleinian school (particularly Money-Kyrle and Segal). The argument is situated within a frame of reference which views the psychoanalytic contribution as part of what the author terms an interdisciplinary conversation. The paper concludes with some more general considerations regarding the horrors of our contemporary world.

[[RunningHead]]

Autoimmunity and Its Expression in the Analytic Situation: A Contemporary Reflection on Our Inherent Self-Destructiveness

Nancy Winters

Nancy Winters

Portland, USA
nancycwinters@gmail.com 2250 NW Flanders St., Ste 212A, OR 97210

Portland, USA

Nancy Winters  

Metapsychology; immunology; psychosomatics

This article explores the psychical expression of autoimmunity in the analytic situation; it is informed by biological theories of autoimmunity in contemporary immunology. Several of my patients developed autoimmune conditions in the course of their analyses, leading me to consider the role of psychical change in disrupting one’s somatopsychic equilibrium. In the psychoanalytic literature, autoimmunity is used metaphorically for attack against what is foreign or unwanted in ourselves. Contemporary immunology, however, suggests a somewhat different metaphor. “Self-reactive” cells present in the body may attack self unless suppressed by inhibitory mechanisms. Biologically, limited self-destructiveness is necessary, but in autoimmune disease it becomes excessive and pathological. I suggest that the presence of biological “self-reactivity” implies an inherent self-destructiveness in line with Freud’s thinking on the death instinct; the notion that this self-destructiveness also has adaptive aspects may contribute to our understanding of Freud’s ideas. In three vignettes I illustrate clinical application of a modified metaphor of autoimmunity, finding that not only is the fusion of life and death instincts important for psychic development, but so is defusion when contained within the analytic relationship. The analytic task is to restore the balance between constructive and pathological self-destructiveness. Implications regarding controversies in psychosomatic theory are briefly considered.

responseSet.title: The Correspondence between Albert Einstein and Sigmund Freud in “Why War” and the Socio-Political Perspective of Alfred Adler
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Correspondence between Albert Einstein and Sigmund Freud in “Why War” and the Socio-Political Perspective of Alfred Adler

Kristin White

Kristin White

kristin.white@t-online.de

Kristin White  

Death drive, striving for power, community feeling, narcissistic defences, guilt, mourning

This paper discusses the correspondence between Albert Einstein and Sigmund Freud, published in the Standard Edition of Freud's writings under the title of “Why War”. Freud's answers to some of Einstein's questions are compared to Alfred Adler's ideas on the role of “striving for power” versus “community feeling” and the role of these two forces in the development of war. Adler had begun to develop an object relations line of thinking in his early papers on the aggressive drive and the need for affection (Adler 1908a and 1908b). It is suggested that if Freud and Adler had been able to continue working together, they might have been able to bring their differing perspectives on the issue of war together to address both the role of power and loss of power, as well as the role of narcissistic defences in the development of war. As it is, this was left to later psychoanalytic thinkers, in particular the Kleinian analysts, who underlined the role of reverting to paranoid-schizoid thinking in the face of humiliation, rather than facing depression and the work of mourning. The work of mourning is illustrated using excerpts from Benjamin Britten's “War Requiem”.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Understanding of the Request for Assisted Suicide
responseSet.authorMast: Stephen Briggs,  Reinhard Lindner,  Mark J. Goldblatt,  Nestor Kapustad and  Martin Teisinge
responseSet.authorCitation: Briggs, S., Lindner, R., Goldblatt, M. J., Kapustad, N. & Teisinge, M.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Understanding of the Request for Assisted Suicide

Stephen Briggs , Reinhard Lindner , Mark Goldblatt , Nestor Kapustad and Martin Teisinge

Martin Teisinge

s.briggs@exeter.ac.uk
Exeter, EX4 4QG, UK
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-4966-7503

Kassel, Germany

Cambridge, MA, USA

Vienna, Austria

Kassel, Germany

Martin Teisinge  

Assisted suicide, euthanasia, terminal illness, palliative care, depression, suicide

The legalisation of assisted dying, including euthanasia and physician assisted suicide, is increasing in countries across the world and constitutes a key contemporary debate, reflecting social changes, in which two views of suicide conflict; that (1) rational reasons justify assisted suicide, providing dignity and control of terminal illness and (2) suicidal wishes are driven by unconscious and disturbing internal conflicts. In this paper we explore the unconscious motives and meanings of requests for assisted suicide. Although there is a paucity of psychoanalytic literature on the subject, and an absence of practice examples, we make two links, firstly, with the literature of palliative and end of life care, and, secondly, with psychoanalytic understanding of suicide, in order to develop the view that unconscious factors are crucial to understanding requests for assisted suicide. We provide an illustrative case example of psychodynamic psychotherapy with a 94-year-old woman, drawing out theoretical and practice implications. We show that unconscious factors and motives lie behind apparently rational requests for assisted suicide, and attention to these through psychoanalytically informed treatment can bring about therapeutic change.
